text: What an excellent horse do they lose, for want of address and boldness to manage him!
author: Alexander the Great
source: Statement upon seeing Bucephalas being led away as useless and beyond training, as quoted in Lives by Plutarch , as translated by Arthur Hugh Clough

text: Know ye not that the end and object of conquest is to avoid doing the same thing as the conquered?
author: Alexander the Great
source: As quoted in Lives by Plutarch , VII, "Demosthenes and Cicero. Alexander and Caesar" (40.2), as translated by Bernadotte Perrin

text: I find no joy in this victory; I would rather have you all standing here, united by language, blood, and vision.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Addressing the dead Hellenes (the Athenean and Thebean Greeks) of the Battle of Chaeronea , as quoted in Historiae Alexandri Magni by Quintus Curtius Rufus

text: If I were not Alexander, I should wish to be Diogenes .
author: Alexander the Great
source: After Diogenes of Sinope who was lying in the sun, responded to a query by Alexander asking if he could do anything for him with a reply requesting that he stop blocking his sunlight. As quoted in "On the Fortune of Alexander" by Plutarch , 332 a-b

text: My purpose is to unite Hellenic and barbarian, to civilize every continent; thus, I cannot be Diogenes, but must follow Herakles.
author: Alexander the Great
source: As quoted in "On the Fortune of Alexander" by Plutarch , 332 a-b

text: Our enemies are soft and enslaved; we are hardened free men, fighting for Greece, led by Alexander, not Darius.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Addressing his troops prior to the Battle of Issus , as quoted in Anabasis Alexandri by Arrian Book II, 7

text: Your ancestors harmed Hellas; now, as leader of the Greeks, I have come to Asia to punish the Persians.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Alexander's letter to Persian king Darius III of Persia in response to a truce plea, as quoted in Anabasis Alexandri by Arrian ; translated as Anabasis of Alexander by P. A. Brunt, for the "Loeb Edition" Book II 14, 4

text: Greeks, join me! Let us move against the barbarians and liberate ourselves from Persian bondage; we should not be slaves.
author: Alexander the Great
source: As quoted in the Historia Alexandri Magni of Pseudo-Kallisthenes , 1.15.1-4

text: You fear punishment, but I will free you, that you may see the difference between a Greek king and a barbarian tyrant. A king does not kill messengers.
author: Alexander the Great
source: As quoted in the Historia Alexandri Magni of Pseudo-Kallisthenes , 1.37.9-13

text: Are you still to learn that the end and perfection of our victories is to avoid the vices and infirmities of those whom we subdue?
author: Alexander the Great
source: As quoted in Lives by Plutarch , as translated by Arthur Hugh Clough

text: There is nothing impossible to him who will try.
author: Alexander the Great
source: On taking charge of an attack on a fortress, in Pushing to the Front, or, Success under Difficulties : A Book of Inspiration (1896) by Orison Swett Marden, p. 55

text: I consider not what Parmenion should receive, but what Alexander should give.
author: Alexander the Great
source: On his gifts for the services of others, as quoted in Dictionary of Phrase and Fable: Giving the Derivation, Source, or Origin of Common Phrases, Allusions, and Words That Have A Tale To Tell (1905) by Ebenezer Cobham Brewer, p. 30

text: Sex and sleep alone make me conscious that I am mortal.
author: Alexander the Great
source: As quoted in Alexander the Great (1973) by Robin Lane Fox

text: Shall I pass by and leave you lying there because of the expedition you led against Greece, or shall I set you up again because of your magnanimity and your virtues in other respects?
author: Alexander the Great
source: Pausing and addressing to a fallen statue of Xerxes the Great

text: A city, like a newborn babe, cannot thrive without the nourishment of fields and their fruits.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Vitruvius , De Architectura Bk. 2, Introduction, Sec. 3

text: For my part, I assure you, I had rather excel others in the knowledge of what is excellent, than in the extent of my power and dominion.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Quoted by Plutarch in Life of Alexander from Plutarch's Lives as translated by John Dryden (1683)

text: An army of sheep led by a lion is better than an army of lions led by a sheep.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Attributed to Alexander, as quoted in The British Battle Fleet: Its Inception and Growth Throughout the Centuries to the Present Day (1915) by Frederick Thomas Jane, but many variants of similar statements exist which have been attributed to others, though in research done for Wikiquote definite citations of original documents have not yet been found for any of them:

text: Alexander was like a cloud in spring or summer, passing over kings, raining upon them, and disappearing—indeed, a summer's cloud disappears very soon.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Abu'l-Fazl Bayhaqi , Tarikh-i Bayhaqi , Book VI, edited by Ali-Akbar Fayyaz , pages 118-119; in context of praising the Ghaznavid kings

text: Alexander prayed for harmony, that Macedonians and Persians should enjoy partnership in government, sharing the same bowl and libations.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Arrian in Anabasis Alexandri , 7.2.6-9

text: You kings have playmates: Darius his Persians, and you, Alexander, your Macedonians and Greeks.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Dio Chrysostom , “Orationes”, 4.48

text: Alexander was the first of the Greeks to take Egypt, and so became the first both of Greeks and of barbarians.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Historia Alexandri Magni of Pseudo-Kallisthenes , 2.4

text: Without justice, what are kingdoms but great robberies? As a captured pirate once retorted to Alexander the Great: 'Because I do it with a small ship, I am called a robber; because you do it with a great fleet, you are called an emperor.'
author: Alexander the Great
source: Justice being taken away, then, what are kingdoms but great robberies? ... Indeed, that was an apt and true reply which was given to Alexander the Great by a pirate who had been seized. For when that king had asked the man what he meant by keeping hostile possession of the sea, he answered with bold pride, " What thou meanest by seizing the whole earth; but because I do it with a petty ship, I am called a robber, whilst thou who dost it with a great fleet art styled emperor. " Augustine of Hippo , The City of God , Book IV, Ch. 4

text: Alexander illustrates with startling clarity the ultimate loneliness of supreme power.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Ernst Badian , Studies in Greek and Roman History, Alexander the Great and the Loneliness of Power , 1964 p. 204

text: Alexander the Great, reflecting on his friends degenerating into sloth and luxury, told them that it was a most slavish thing to luxuriate, and a most royal thing to labour.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Isaac Barrow , in "Sermon 51 : Of Industry in General", in Sermons on Various Subjects (1823), Vol. 3. p. 33

text: Alexander of Macedon, upon hearing that there were other worlds, wept that he had not yet conquered one, although soon after the funeral urn sufficed him.
author: Alexander the Great
source: John Calvin , in his interpretation of Psalm 146 in On The Book Of Psalms (1557) as translated by Rev. James Anderson (1849). Note: Alexander never occupied an urn: he was embalmed after death and conveyed to Memphis, whereupon his body was placed in a gold sarcophagus.

text: Nothing remains but to sit down and weep like Alexander, when he wanted other worlds to conquer.
author: Alexander the Great
source: William Congreve , in words for the character Fainall in Way of the World (1700)

text: We are not in the situation of poor Alexander the Great, who wept because there were no more worlds to conquer.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Washington Irving in Salmagundi : Or, The Whim-whams and Opinions of Launcelot Langstaff, Esq. and Others (1835)

text: He, their comrade — their commander, He, the earth's adored — He, the godlike Alexander!
author: Alexander the Great
source: Letitia Elizabeth Landon , "The Death-Bed of Alexander the Great", The New Monthly Magazine , Volume 45, Part 3 (1835), p. 303

text: I have wrestled with Thanatos knee to knee and I know how death is vanquished. Man's immortality is not to live forever; for that wish is born of fear. Each moment free from fear makes a man immortal.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Mary Renault 's portrayal of Alexander in Fire from Heaven (1969)

text: Those who look in mankind only for their own littleness, and make them believe in that, kill more than he ever will in all his wars.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Mary Renault , The Persian Boy (1972)

text: When magic through nerves and reason passes, imagination, force, and passion will thunder. The portrait of the world is changed.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Dejan Stojanovic in Circling, ”Alexander the Great” (Sequence: “A Warden with No Keys”) (1993)

text: Once upon a time, in days of long ago, Alexander the Great complained bitterly that there were no worlds left for him to conquer.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Alfred Wainwright , A Pennine Journey : The Story of a Long Walk in 1938 (1986), p. 1

text: War is an act of violence, compelling the foe to bend to our will.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: War therefore is an act of violence intended to compel our opponent to fulfil our will .

text: War is such a dangerous business that mistakes that come from kindness are the very worst.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 1, Section 3, ¶1

text: To introduce into the philosophy of War itself a principle of moderation would be an absurdity .
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 1, Section 3, ¶3

text: War is an act of violence pushed to its utmost bounds.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 1, Section 3, ¶8

text: War is but the continuation of policy by other means.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: War is merely the continuation of policy by other means. We see, therefore, that war is not merely an act of policy but a true political instrument, a continuation of political intercourse carried on with other means. What remains peculiar to war is simply the peculiar nature of its means.

text: When the cost of war outweighs its political aim, peace must follow.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 2

text: Two qualities are indispensable: the first, a 'coup d'oeil,' the second, unwavering resolution.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: [...] two qualities are indispensable [...] The first is figuratively expressed by the French phrase w:coup d'oeil . The other is resolution.

text: True resolution is courage against responsibility, a 'courage d'esprit' where the intellect must ignite the heart.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Determination in a single instance is an expression of courage ; if it becomes characteristic, a mental habit . But here we are referring not to physical courage but to courage to accept responsibility , courage in the face of a moral danger . This has often been called courage d'esprit , because it is created by the intellect . That, however, does not make it an act of the intellect: it is an act of temperament . Intelligence alone is not courage; we often see that the most intelligent people are irresolute. Since in the rush of events a man is governed by feelings rather than by thought , the intellect needs to arouse the quality of courage, which then supports and sustains it in action . Looked at in this way, the role of determination is to limit the agonies of doubt and the perils of hesitation when the motives for action are inadequate.

text: A strong spirit remains balanced amidst the fiercest emotions, its insight a compass on a storm-tossed ship.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Strength of character does not consist solely in having powerful feelings, but in maintaining one’s balance in spite of them. Even with the violence of emotion, judgment and principle must still function like a ship’s compass, which records the slightest variations however rough the sea .

text: Action in war is like movement in a resistant element. Just as the simplest and most natural of movements, walking, cannot easily be performed in water, so in war it is difficult for normal efforts to achieve even moderate results.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 7, as translated by Michael Howard and Peter Paret (1976)

text: Civilization's advance has done nothing to alter the impulse to destroy the enemy, central to the very idea of war.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: The worst of all conditions in which a belligerent can find himself is to be utterly defenseless.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Men are always more inclined to pitch their estimate of the enemy's strength too high than too low, such is human nature.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Only the element of chance is needed to make war a gamble, and that element is never absent.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: In the whole range of human activities, war most closely resembles a game of cards.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Although our intellect always longs for clarity and certainty, our nature often finds uncertainty fascinating.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: With uncertainty in one scale, courage and self-confidence should be thrown into the other to correct the balance. The greater they are, the greater the margin that can be left for accidents.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Blind aggressiveness would destroy the attack itself, not the defense.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Our discussion has shown that while in war many different roads can lead to the goal, to the attainment of the political object, fighting is the only possible means.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Any complex activity, if it is to be carried on with any degree of virtuosity, calls for appropriate gifts of intellect and temperament. If they are outstanding and reveal themselves in exceptional achievements, their possessor is called a ' genius '.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: To face the unforeseen, an intellect that retains truth's glimmer and the courage to follow its faint light are indispensable.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: The role of determination is to limit the agonies of doubt and the perils of hesitation when the motives for action are inadequate.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Of all the passions that inspire a man in a battle, none, we have to admit, is so powerful and so constant as the longing for honor and renown.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Obstinacy is a fault of temperament. Stubbornness and intolerance of contradiction result from a special kind of egotism, which elevates above everything else the pleasure of its autonomous intellect, to which others must bow.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Self-reliance is the best defense against the pressures of the moment.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Everything in war is simple, but the simplest thing is difficult.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Though material work is precise, aesthetic effect on mind or senses dissolves into vague ideas.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Modern wars are seldom fought without hatred between nations; this serves more or less as a substitute for hatred between individuals.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Soldierly simplicity is the military's best, but higher ranks bring a complex drama of passions.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Talent and genius operate outside the rules, and theory conflicts with practice.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: The more physical the activity, the less the difficulties will be. The more the activity becomes intellectual and turns into motives which exercise a determining influence on the commander's will, the more the difficulties will increase.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Great things alone can make a great mind, and petty things will make a petty mind unless a man rejects them as completely alien.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Knowledge in war is very simple, being concerned with so few subjects, and only with their final results at that. But this does not make its application easy.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Knowledge must be so absorbed into the mind that it ceases to exist in a separate, objective way.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: ...it is better to go on striking in the same direction than to move one's forces this way and that.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: There are times when the utmost daring is the height of wisdom.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Theoretical literature, crammed with jargon, loses its readers at obscure crossroads, becoming hollow shells where the author no longer knows what he thinks.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: To tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth, one must dedicate oneself like a pilgrim, sparing no effort, fearing no power, and rising above all vanity.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Combat is an expression of hostile feelings, and though war may begin as mere intentions, hatred between nations or individuals is an inescapable, fundamental part of it, often stirred up by the violence itself.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: A prince or general can best demonstrate his genius by managing a campaign exactly to suit his objectives and his resources, doing neither too much nor too little.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Where execution is dominant, as it is in the individual events of a war whether great or small, then intellectual factors are reduced to a minimum.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: To view war as isolated events, rather than a chain of linked engagements, is to mistake windfall profits for true value, ignoring how present gains may lead to future disadvantages.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: ...in war, the advantages and disadvantages of a single action could only be determined by the final balance.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Moral elements are paramount in war, permeating its spirit and will; they defy academic classification, demanding to be felt, not counted. To disregard them with rigid rules is paltry philosophy, for genius operates beyond such constraints.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch 3 : Moral Factors, as translated by Michael Howard and Peter Paret.

text: A commander's true talent finds its greatest scope in rough, hilly country, where control is challenging but possible, unlike mountains or open plains.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Boldness will be at a disadvantage only in an encounter with deliberate caution, which may be considered bold in its own right, and is certainly just as powerful and effective; but such cases are rare.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Timidity is the root of prudence in the majority of men.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Boldness governed by superior intellect is the mark of a hero.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: ...as man under pressure tends to give in to physical and intellectual weakness, only great strength of will can lead to the objective.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Beauty cannot be defined by abscissas and ordinates; neither are circles and ellipses created by their geometrical formulas.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: Forces idle while the enemy fights are wasted, a greater sin than misuse.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: ...any move made in a state of tension will be of more important, and will have more results, than it would have made in a state of equilibrium. In times of maximum tension this importance will rise to an infinite degree.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: The state of crisis is the real war; the equilibrium is nothing but its reflex.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: All war presupposes human weakness and seeks to exploit it.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Unknown

text: What is the object of defense? To preserve. To preserve is easier than to acquire.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 1

text: Every suspension of offensive action, either from erroneous views, from fear or from indolence, is in favor of the side acting defensively.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 1

text: If defense is the stronger form of war, yet has a negative object, it follows that it should be used only so long as weakness compels, and be abandoned as soon as we are strong enough to pursue a positive object.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 1

text: If an assailant bypasses a defensive position with impunity, he will immediately enforce its abandonment and end its usefulness.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 1

text: Surprise is effective when we suddenly face the enemy with unexpected numerical superiority at one point: it is the most powerful medium in the art of war.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 1

text: Phillipsburg was the name of one of those badly drawn fortresses resembling a fool with his nose too close to the wall.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 2

text: The Conqueror is always a lover of peace: he would prefer to take over our country unopposed.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 2

text: A conqueror is always a lover of peace; he would prefer to enter our state unopposed. To prevent this, the weak must always be armed.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 5

text: A general who allows himself to be decisively defeated in an extended mountain position deserves to be court-martialled.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 11

text: ...only a fraction of book learning will seep into practical life anyhow; and the more foolish the theory, the less of it.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch. 17

text: His is not simply the greatest but the only truly great book on war.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Bernard Brodie , ‘The Continuing Relevance of On War ’, in Carl von Clausewitz, On War , edited and translated by Michael Howard and Peter Paret (1976; 1989), p. 53

text: Far from being an exact science, war is a dreadful and impassioned drama.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ferdinand Foch , The Principles of War (1918), pp. 4-5

text: War is a continuation of policy by other means.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Vladimir Lenin , ' War and Revolution: A Lecture Delivered May 14 (27), 1917 ', Pravda No. 93 (23 April 1929) Известно изречение одного из самых знаменитых писателей по философии войн и по истории войн — Клаузевица, которое гласит: «Война есть продолжение политики иными средствами». Это изречение принадлежит писателю, который обозревал историю войн и выводил философские уроки из этой истории — вскоре после эпохи наполеоновских войн. Этот писатель, основные мысли которого сделались в настоящее время безусловным приобретением всякого мыслящего человека, уже около 80 лет тому назад боролся против обывательского и невежественного предрассудка, будто бы войну можно выделить из политики соответственных правительств, соответственных классов, будто бы войну когда-нибудь можно рассматривать как простое нападение, нарушающее мир, и затем восстановление этого нарушенного мира.

text: He was the source of 'absolute war,' a theory that, beginning with 'war is only a continuation of state policy by other means,' ultimately made policy the slave of strategy.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: B. H. Liddell Hart , The Ghost of Napoleon (1933), p. 120

text: Clausewitz's principle of force without limit, without calculation of cost, is fit only for a hate-maddened mob; it negates statesmanship and intelligent strategy.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: B. H. Liddell Hart , The Ghost of Napoleon (1933), p. 122

text: [T]he rascal has a ‘common sense’ bordering on wit.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Karl Marx to Friedrich Engels , quoted in Carl von Clausewitz, On War , ed. Michael Howard and Peter Paret (1989), p. 44

text: Clausewitz was right: 'Concentrate against the main enemy, who must be overthrown first,' for 'the armed forces form the true objective.'
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: George Orwell , review of B. H. Liddell Hart's The British Way in Warfare in New Statesman and Nation (21 November 1942), quoted in George Orwell, The Collected Essays, Journalism and Letters of George Orwell. Volume II: My Country Right or Left, 1940–1943 , eds. Sonia Orwell and Ian Angus (1968), p. 248

text: Foch emphasized enemy demoralization; Clausewitz, more coolly, saw the capture or killing of the enemy as the final destruction of their ability to fight, moral overthrow being only temporary.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Walther Reinhardt , quoted in Herbert Rosinski, The German Army (1939; rev. ed., 1944), pp. 185-186

text: Clausewitz was a rare combination: philosophical contemplation and impatience for action, cold, lucid intellect and passionate sensitivity—a reflective spirit ardently longing for heroic deeds.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Gerhard Ritter , The Sword and the Sceptre: The Problem of Militarism in Germany, Volume I: The Prussian Tradition, 1740–1890 (1972), p. 52

text: From his frustration, Clausewitz produced 'On War,' the most profound, comprehensive, and systematic examination of war that has appeared to the present day.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Herbert Rosinski , The German Army (1939; rev. ed., 1944), p. 73

text: Clausewitz warned: 'The danger is that a style of war, developed out of a single case, can easily outlive the situation that gave rise to it; theory should prevent this by lucid, rational criticism.'
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Leon Trotsky , ' Military Doctrine or Pseudo-Military Doctrinairism ' (1921), The Military Writings of Leon Trotsky, Volume 5: 1921–1923

text: [Clausewitz] quite understands that you must break the enemy's will, for which he suggests three processes: first to crush his army, and then to take his capital and, if that is not enough, to occupy his territory.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Spenser Wilkinson to B. H. Liddell Hart (14 June 1924), quoted in Brian Bond, Liddell Hart: A Study of His Military Thought (1977), p. 44

text: I wonder if I could have been here before as I drive up the Roman road the Theater seems familiar — perhaps I headed a legion up that same white road... I passed a chateau in ruins which I possibly helped escalade in the middle ages. There is no proof nor yet any denial. We were, We are, and we will be.
author: George S. Patton
source: Indicating some of his speculations about reincarnation , in a letter to his mother from Chamlieu, France during World War I (20 November 1917)

text: Through the travail of the ages, Midst the pomp and toil of war , Have I fought and strove and perished Countless times upon this star . In the form of many people In all panoplies of time Have I seen the luring vision Of the Victory Maid, sublime.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: I have sinned and I have suffered, played the hero and the knave; fought for belly, shame, or country, and for each have found a grave.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: I have fought with gun and cutlass On the red and slippery deck With all Hell aflame within me And a rope around my neck.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: I fought in many guises, many names, but always me, dying to be born a fighter, to die again, once more.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: Wars may be fought with weapons, but they are won by men . It is the spirit of the men who follow and of the man who leads that gains the victory .
author: George S. Patton
source: Cavalry Journal (September 1933)

text: Sometimes I think your life and mine are under the protection of some supreme being or fate , because, after many years of parallel thought, we find ourselves in the positions we now occupy.
author: George S. Patton
source: Letter to Dwight D. Eisenhower (May 1942), as quoted in Eisenhower : A Soldier's Life (2003) by Carlo D'Este, p. 301

text: It is interesting how you get used to death; you become so used to it you never think about it.
author: George S. Patton
source: Letter to Frederick Ayer (5 May 1943), published in The Patton Papers 1940-1945 (1996) edited by Martin Blumenson, p. 243

text: I find that moral courage is the most valuable and most usually absent characteristic.
author: George S. Patton
source: In a letter to Beatrice (22 August 1943), published in The Patton Papers 1940-1945 (1996) edited by Martin Blumenson

text: A pint of sweat will save a gallon of blood.
author: George S. Patton
source: Letter (3 March 1944), later published in War As I Knew It (1947) Similar expressions were also used in his famous "Speech to the Third Army" in June 1944. The phrase is similar to one attributed to Erwin Rommel , "Sweat saves blood, blood saves lives, and brains saves both", and to an even older one by August Willich: "A drop of sweat on the drill ground will save many drops of blood on the battlefield" from The Army: Standing Army or National Army? (1866)

text: My flanks are something for the enemy to worry about, not me.
author: George S. Patton
source: Conference with his officers (1 August 1944), as quoted in General Patton : A Soldiers Life (2002) by Stanley P. Hirshon, p. 502

text: Son, only a pimp in a Louisiana whore-house carries pearl-handled revolvers. These are ivory.
author: George S. Patton
source: Remarks to a reporter when asked about his "pearl-handled revolvers"

text: Americans love to fight, traditionally. All real Americans love the sting and clash of battle.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: Americans love a winner, despise cowards, and play to win all the time; the very idea of losing is hateful.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: The real hero fights even though he is scared, for battle is the most magnificent competition, bringing out all that is best and removing all that is base.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: Remember that the enemy is just as frightened as you are, and probably more so. They are not supermen.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: Alertness must be bred into every soldier; I don't give a fuck for a man who's not always on his toes.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: There are four hundred neatly marked graves somewhere in Sicily. All because one man went to sleep on the job. But they are German graves, because we caught the bastard asleep before they did.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: An Army is a team; this individual heroic stuff is pure horse shit.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: We have the finest food, the finest equipment, the best spirit , and the best men in the world. Why, by God , I actually pity those poor sons-of-bitches we're going up against. By God, I do.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: My men don't surrender. I don't want to hear of any soldier under my command being captured unless he has been hit. Even if you are hit, you can still fight back.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: If you put the letter "S" in front of Hitler , then you have my opinion of him.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: Every single man in this Army plays a vital role; don't ever think your job is unimportant.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: Kill off the Goddamned cowards and we will have a nation of brave men.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: Let the first bastards to find out be the Goddamned Germans, and hear them howl, 'Jesus Christ, it's the Goddamned Third Army again and that son-of-a-fucking-bitch Patton!'
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: The shortest way home is through Berlin and Tokyo; when we get to Berlin, I am personally going to shoot that paper hanging son-of-a-bitch Hitler, just like I'd shoot a snake!
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: My men don't dig foxholes; foxholes only slow up an offensive. We're going to rip out their living Goddamned guts and use them to grease the treads of our tanks.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: We are advancing constantly and are not interested in holding onto anything, except the enemy's balls. We are going to twist his balls and kick the living shit out of him.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: An ounce of sweat will save a gallon of blood; the harder we push, the fewer of our men will be killed.
author: George S. Patton
source: Unknown

text: Twenty years from now, you can look your grandson straight in the eye and say, 'Son, your Granddaddy rode with the Great Third Army and a Son-of-a-Goddamned-Bitch named Georgie Patton!'
author: George S. Patton
source: Though 2 publications, Eyewitness to War (2006) by Antony Bird and Nicholas Bird, p. 256, and Charge! : History's Greatest Military Speeches‎ (2007) by Steve Israel, p. 200, have been found which use "George", all earlier published sources available use "Georgie" in this line.

text: Have taken Trier with two divisions. What do you want me to do? Give it back?
author: George S. Patton
source: 2 March 1945 reply to a message from General Dwight Eisenhower to bypass the German city of Trier because it would take four divisions to capture it, as quoted in the Introduction to War as I Knew it (1947) by George Smith Patton, Jr., with Paul Donal Harkins , p. 20

text: I don't know what you think you're trying to do, but the krauts ought to pin a medal on you for helping them mess up discipline for us.
author: George S. Patton
source: During a March 1945 meeting with Bill Mauldin , complaining about his "Willy and Joe" cartoons; as quoted in The Brass Ring (1971) by Bill Mauldin

text: It is a popular idea that a man is a hero just because he was killed in action. Rather, I think, a man is frequently a fool when he gets killed.
author: George S. Patton
source: Speech at the Hatch Memorial Shell, Boston, Massachusetts (7 June 1945), quoted in The Last Days of Patton (1981), p. 85, by Ladislas Farago and ' The Patton Papers: 1940-1945 (1974), p. 721, edited by Martin Blumenson.

text: It is foolish and wrong to mourn the men who died. Rather we should thank God that such men lived.
author: George S. Patton
source: Speech at the Copley Plaza Hotel, Boston, Massachusetts (7 June 1945), quoted in Patton : Ordeal and Triumph (1970) by Ladislas Farago

text: The more I see of people, the more I regret that I survived the war.
author: George S. Patton
source: when ordered to hand over German prisoners to the French for forced labor, per page 641 of "Brothers, Rivals, Victors" by Jonathan W. Jordan

text: Never tell people how to do things. Tell them what to do, and they will surprise you with their ingenuity .
author: George S. Patton
source: War As I Knew It (1947) "Reflections and Suggestions"

text: There is a great deal of talk about loyalty from the bottom to the top. Loyalty from the top down is even more necessary and much less prevalent. One of the most frequently noted characteristics of great men who have remained great is loyalty to their subordinates.
author: George S. Patton
source: War As I Knew It (1947); also quoted in Patton's One-Minute Messages: Tactical Leadership Skills for Business Management (1995) by Charles M. Province, p. 88

text: A good plan violently executed right now is far better than a perfect plan executed next week.
author: George S. Patton
source: War as I knew it (1947), as cited in Oxford Essential Quotations (4th ed.) , By Susan Ratcliffe .

text: Between the plan and the operation, there is always an unknown; that's where God and prayer come in.
author: George S. Patton
source: As quoted in "The True Story of The Patton Prayer" by James H. O'Neill in Review of the News (6 October 1971)

text: My men can eat their belts, but my tanks have gotta have gas.
author: George S. Patton
source: On the gasoline supplies for his tanks, as quoted in The Struggle for Europe‎ (1972) by Chester Wilmot, p. 473

text: Someday we will have to fight them and it will take six years and cost us six million lives.
author: George S. Patton
source: As quoted in The Unknown Patton (1983) by Charles M. Province, p. 100

text: A good solution applied with vigor now is better than a perfect solution applied ten minutes later.
author: George S. Patton
source: As quoted in The Unknown Patton (1983) by Charles M. Province, p. 165

text: An army without profanity couldn't fight its way out of a piss-soaked paper bag.
author: George S. Patton
source: Remark to his nephew about his copious profanity, quoted in The Unknown Patton (1983) by Charles M. Province, p. 184

text: Always do everything you ask of those you command.
author: George S. Patton
source: As quoted in I Remember General Patton's Principles (1984) by Porter B. Williamson, p. 174

text: Accept the challenges , so that you may feel the exhilaration of victory.
author: George S. Patton
source: As quoted in Textbook of Phacoemulsification (1988) by William F. Maloney and Lincoln Grindle, p. 79

text: We herd sheep, we drive cattle, we lead people . Lead me, follow me, or get out of my way.
author: George S. Patton
source: As quoted in Pocket Patriot : Quotes from American Heroes (2005) edited by Kelly Nickell, p. 157

text: There is only one tactical principle which is not subject to change . It is to use the means at hand to inflict the maximum amount of wound, death , and destruction on the enemy in the minimum amount of time .
author: George S. Patton
source: As quoted in Liberalism is a Mental Disorder : Savage Solutions‎ (2005) by Michael Savage , Ch. 1 : More Patton, Less Patent Leather, p. 4

text: Fixed fortifications are a monument to the stupidity of man.
author: George S. Patton
source: Quoted in 50 Military Leaders Who Changed the World‎ (2007) by William Weir, p. 173

text: It is the cold glitter of the attacker's eye not the point of the questing bayonet that breaks the line.
author: George S. Patton
source: Quoted in How We Are Changed by War: A Study of Letters and Diaries from Colonial Conflicts to Operation Iraqi Freedom (2010) by D.C. Gill, p. 70

text: Don't fight a battle if you don't gain anything by winning.
author: George S. Patton
source: This is cited to Patton in Patton's Principles : A Handbook for Managers Who Mean It! (1982) by Porter B. Williamson as well as Leadership (1990) by William Safire and Leonard Safir, p. 47, but is also cited to Erwin Rommel‎ from his Infanterie Greift An [ Infantry Attacks ] (1937) in World War II : The Definitive Visual History (2009) by Richard Holmes, p. 128, and Timelines of History (2011) by DK Publishing, p. 392

text: Almighty Father, grant us fair weather for Battle, that we may advance from victory to victory.
author: George S. Patton
source: Though Patton commissioned this prayer and ordered 250,000 copies of it printed with his signature, it was actually composed by Chief Chaplain James H. O'Neill Review of the News (6 October 1971)

text: Fail to honor people, They fail to honor you; But of a good leader, who talks little, When his work is done, his aims fulfilled, They will all say, We did this ourselves.
author: George S. Patton
source: This is actually a translation of a statement by Lao Zi from the Tao Te Ching (Daodejing) . Patton may have used a similar or identical expression, perhaps quoting the book.

text: Give me an army of West Point graduates, I'll win a battle. Give me a handful of Texas Aggies and I'll win a war!
author: George S. Patton
source: Mike Province, founder and president of The Patton Society calls this an urban legend and in the Texas A&M Battalion (2 October 2006) is quoted as saying "I've gotten e-mails and questions regarding that quote for several years... People will use it with Texas Aggies, The Citadel, Virginia Military Institute and even Clemson. All of these schools want to be linked to Patton... Anything is possible... I honestly don't believe he said it, because I've heard too many people say that he said it about their school. But if anyone out there can find proof that he said it, I'd love to hear about it and get it out there." If any school has a claim, it is the Virginia Military Institute; Patton's grandfather, grand-uncles, and his father all were VMI graduates. Patton himself spent a year at VMI before going to West Point. VMI has many George Patton relics donated by his family in its museum. Please also note that the photo of Patton as a cadet has him wearing a VMI coatee and cap.

text: I'd rather have a German division in front of me, than a French one behind.
author: George S. Patton
source: Misattributed by former Defense Secretary James Schlesinger on Fox News. Patton commanded French troops, the 2nd Armored Division commanded by Philippe Leclerc, integrated in the Third Army, and had rocky but friendly relations with the French general. For instance, on August, 15 1944 Patton wrote in his diary: "Leclerc came in very much excited. He said, among other things, that if he were not allowed to advance on Paris, he would resign. I told him in my best French that he was a baby and said I had left him in the most dangerous place on the front. We parted friends"

text: I want you to remember that no bastard ever won a war by dying for his country. He won it by making the other poor, dumb bastard die for his country.
author: George S. Patton
source: Spoken by George C. Scott in the film Patton .

text: Rommel, you magnificent bastard! I read your book!
author: George S. Patton
source: Spoken by George C. Scott in the film Patton , portraying his defeat of what he thought were forces under the command of Erwin Rommel ; however, the book portrayed in that film is purely fictional — Rommel never finished the book he was writing on tank warfare, but did write a book on his experiences in WW I . It was widely read, regarded a classic of modern military tactics, and published in abbreviated form for study by US army officers.

text: I don't see the wonder in wonder weapons. Killing without heroics, nothing is glorified, nothing reaffirmed. No heroes, no cowards, no troops, no generals; only those left alive, and those left dead. I'm glad I won't live to see it.
author: George S. Patton
source: Attributed as a quote in Charles W. Hudlin, "Morality and the Military Profession: Problems and Solutions", Military Ethics (National Defense University Press, 1987) ; but Hudlin cites the biographical dramatization Patton (1970 film) which does not purport to use Patton's actual words.

text: Men are at war with each other because each man is at war with himself.
author: George S. Patton
source: This is almost always attributed to US Ambassador Francis Meehan , though without citations, and only very rarely to Patton.

text: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death I shall fear no evil, because I am the meanest son-of-a-bitch in the valley.
author: George S. Patton
source: This was a widely published anonymous derivative of Psalm 23 which arose in the early 1970s on wall-posters, plaques and t-shirts, with an early variant also reading "Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death I shall fear no evil: for I am the meanest bastard in the valley"; much cruder variants, with less clear association with the original biblical passages have since emerged on the internet, and in very recent years have begun to be attributed to Patton. There are no historical sources indicating he ever actually said anything resembling this.

text: For Patton, leadership was never simply about making plans and giving orders, it was about transforming oneself into a symbol.
author: George S. Patton
source: Alan Axelrod , Patton: A Biography (2006), p. 80-82

text: Arrogant, publicity-seeking, and personally flawed, Patton was among the greatest generals of the war.
author: George S. Patton
source: Terry Brighton, Patton, Montgomery, Rommel: Masters of War (2009), p. xv

text: I prefer to remember Patton as a man, with all the frailties and faults of a human being, whose greatness is therefore all the more of a triumph.
author: George S. Patton
source: Omar Bradley , in his memoirs A Soldier's Story (1951) p. xii.

text: Profane yet reverent, imperious yet humble, Patton saw war as a holy crusade.
author: George S. Patton
source: Omar Bradley , A Soldier's Story (1951), p. 52

text: Patton, though a successful commander, had not learned to command himself; he drove subordinates with bombast and threats, achieving results but not affection.
author: George S. Patton
source: Omar Bradley , A Soldier's Story (1951), p. 52

text: General George Patton, who saw the Nazi concentration camps, likened 'this Nazi thing' to 'a Democratic and Republican election fight' and privately described the Jews as 'lower than animals.'
author: George S. Patton
source: Elly Bulkin essay (1985)

text: To be a successful soldier you must know history.
author: George S. Patton
source: Carlo D'Este, Eisenhower: A Soldier's Life (2002), p. 45-46

text: Patton is indispensable to the war effort – one of the guarantors of our victory.
author: George S. Patton
source: Dwight D. Eisenhower , as quoted in Patton: A Genius for War (1995) by Carlo D'Este, p. 536

text: Patton, convinced of an inevitable confrontation with the Soviet Union, declared, 'If you wanted Moscow, I could give it to you.'
author: George S. Patton
source: Winston Groom, The Generals: Patton, MacArthur, Marshall, and the Winning of World War II (2015), p. 394-395

text: Patton, asked about SS prisoners, replied: 'SS means no more in Germany than being a Democrat in America.'
author: George S. Patton
source: Winston Groom, The Generals: Patton, MacArthur, Marshall, and the Winning of World War II (2015), p. 395

text: Patton in the flesh was an enigma.
author: George S. Patton
source: S. L. A. Marshall , on Patton and his portrayal in Patton (1970) , in "Great Georgie Redone", in The Charleston Gazette (21 March 1970), § 4. p. 4

text: If you're a leader, you don't push wet spaghetti, you pull it. Patton understood this, and though crazy, his techniques got men out of their foxholes.
author: George S. Patton
source: Bill Mauldin , in The Brass Ring (1971)

text: Lincoln's remark after they got after Grant comes to mind when I think of Patton – 'I can't spare this man, he fights'.
author: George S. Patton
source: John J. McCloy, as quoted Eisenhower: A Soldier's Life (2002) by Carlo D'Este, p. 442. McCloy said this to Eisenhower while he was Assistant Secretary of War during World War II.

text: Patton, despite his image, fanatically disapproved of taking casualties, yet intensely desired to inflict them upon the enemy.
author: George S. Patton
source: George C. Scott, who portrayed Patton in the 1970 film of the same name , as quoted in The Fighting Pattons (1997) by Brian M. Sobel, p. 136

text: You can't be a leader without respect; a good leader is respected even by their enemies, like Patton and Rommel.
author: George S. Patton
source: Robert J. Shano, Never Too Old For War (2023), self-published, paperback first edition, p. 48

text: Beyond his swashbuckling image, Patton possessed 'civil courage' to speak his mind and a profound scholarly knowledge of strategy.
author: George S. Patton
source: Albert C. Wedemeyer, Wedemeyer Reports! (1958), p. 222

text: Be careful what you say. No matter what, they'll put it in the newspapers.
author: George S. Patton
source: William Westmoreland , in his memoirs A Soldier Reports (1976), p. 22.

text: Never tell people how to do things, tell them what to do, and they will surprise you with their ingenuity.
author: George S. Patton
source: Chester Wilmot, The Struggle for Europe (1952), p. 465-466

text: Successful leaders must be highly visible, sharing hardships, and always appear to move forward, never backward.
author: George S. Patton
source: Richard Winters , in his book Beyond Band of Brothers: The War Memoirs of Major Dick Winters (2006), p. 286

text: The art of concentrating strength at one point, forcing a breakthrough, rolling up and securing the flanks on either side, and then penetrating like lightning , before the enemy has time to react, deep into his rear.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Strategies he promoted which have been called Blitzkrieg (Lightning War), as quoted in Europe Since 1914 (1966) by Gordon Alexander Craig

text: The German soldier has astonished the world; the Italian Bersagliere has astonished the German soldier.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: On the plaque dedicated to the Bersaglieri that fought at Mersa Matruh and Alamein.

text: One must not judge everyone in the world by his qualities as a soldier: otherwise we should have no civilization.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: As quoted in Dirty Little Secrets : Military Information You're Not Supposed To Know (1990) by James F. Dunnigan and Albert A. Nofi, p. 50

text: Gentlemen, you have fought like lions and been led by donkeys .
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Said to captured British officers during the Siege of Tobruk , as quoted in The Guinness History of the British Army (1993) by John Pimlott, p. 138

text: Be an example to your men in duty and private life; never spare yourself, and let them see your endurance of fatigue and privation.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Address as Director of the Military School in Wiener Neustadt at the passing out parade of the 1938 class of cadets.

text: Better to wield the spade too much than too little; such labor saves blood.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Better too much spade work than too little! This work saves blood. p. 28.

text: In equal combat, victory belongs to him with one more cartridge in the chamber.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: “In a man on man fight between comparable opponents, the winner is he who has put one more bullet through the barrel.” p. 62.

text: When victory is decisive, exploit it; for it is in the pursuit of a broken foe that true triumph is forged.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Ch. V : Graziani's Defeat - Cause and Effect, p. 96.

text: Mortal danger is an effective antidote for fixed ideas.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Ch. XI : The Initiative Passes, p. 244.

text: To fight an enemy with complete air command is to fight like a savage against modern troops.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Ch XIII : Now or Never - Alam Halfa, p. 285.

text: Far from the battle makes old soldiers.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Ch XV : Alamein in Retrospect, p. 327.

text: But courage which goes against military expediency is stupidity, or, if it is insisted upon by a commander, irresponsibility.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Ch XVI : The Great Retreat, p. 347.

text: Don't fight a battle if you don't gain anything by winning.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: This is cited to to Rommel‎'s Infanterie Greift An [ Infantry Attacks ] (1937) in World War II : The Definitive Visual History (2009) by Richard Holmes, p. 128, and Timelines of History (2011) by DK Publishing, p. 392, but to George S. Patton , in Patton's Principles : A Handbook for Managers Who Mean It! (1982) by Porter B. Williamson as well as Leadership (1990) by William Safire and Leonard Safir, p. 47

text: We have a very daring and skilful opponent against us, and, may I say across the havoc of war, a great general.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Winston Churchill , in The Second World War , Vol. 3: The Grand Alliance (1950), pp. 176-177.

text: Inactivity is seldom forgiven a general by fate.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: David William Fraser , Knight's Cross : A Life of Field Marshal Erwin Rommel‎ (1994), p. 562

text: Though victory is paramount, it is not the sole measure of military genius; for war is both business and art.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: David William Fraser , Knight's Cross : A Life of Field Marshal Erwin Rommel‎ (1994), p. 562

text: Until I delved into Rommel's own papers I regarded him as a brilliant tactician and great fighting leader, but did not realize how deep a sense of strategy he had — or at any rate, developed in reflection.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Basil Henry Liddell Hart , in The Rommel Papers (1982), p. xv

text: Rommel excelled with fast-moving troops up to army level, but his volatile moods and limited strategic scope made higher command too much.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Albert Kesselring , to Leon Goldensohn (4 February 1946)

text: Rommel, a paragon of chivalry, defied Hitler's brutal orders, treating Allied prisoners with humanity and embodying 'War Without Hate'.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Steven Pressfield , in Killing Rommel (2009), p. 7

text: Rommel, a legend of military genius and chivalry, was Germany's finest general, whose desert campaigns nearly turned the tide of war.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Steven Pressfield , in "Leaders with Character, Chivalry and Courage – Relics of the Past?" at Knol

text: He was ordered several times by Hitler to "Stand and Die." To fight to the last bullet, the last man. To execute and torture prisoners. He defied those orders.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Steven Pressfield , in "Leaders with Character, Chivalry and Courage – Relics of the Past?" at Knol

text: Rommel had a feel for the battlefield like no other man.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: U.S. General Norman Schwarzkopf , commander of coalition forces, Persian Gulf War, as quoted in Discovering the Rommel Murder (1994) by Charles Marshall ISBN 0-8117-1480-2

text: So legendary was Rommel's 'magic' that Churchill cried, 'What else matters but beating him!'
author: Erwin Rommel
source: C.L. Sulzberger, in his book The American Heritage Picture History of World War II (1966), p. 234

text: Bitter over Hitler's 'victory or death' order, Rommel, against all odds, miraculously slipped his forces from annihilation at El Alamein.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: C.L. Sulzberger, in his book The American Heritage Picture History of World War II (1966), p. 241

text: Rommel was a rare military phenomenon: as brave as Ney, dashing as Murat, and as cool a tactician as Wellington, yet with superior intellect.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Field Marshal Archibald Wavell , as quoted in Discovering the Rommel Murder (1994) by Charles Marshall, p. 1

text: His magnetic personality transformed men into true soldiers; inexhaustible, he instinctively knew the enemy, crafting startling, spontaneous plans.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Theodor Werner, an officer who, during World War I, served under Rommel, as quoted in The Trail of the Fox (1977) by David Irving , p. 15

text: I cannot attribute human flaws to the divine. I believe the benevolent Almighty cares not for human affairs, but rather all events stem from unpredictable causes, before which I humbly confess my ignorance.
author: Frederick the Great
source: I feel the deepest veneration for the divine being, and therefore I am careful not to attribute to him unjust, fickle behavior, which would be condemned by the meanest mortal. Because of that, dear sister, I prefer not to believe that the almighty, benevolent being is at all concerned with human affairs. Rather I do attribute everything that happens to the living beings and certain effects of incalculable causes and I silently bow down before this being which is worthy of adoration, by admitting my ignorance concerning his ways, which his godly wisdom chose not to reveal.

text: I think it better to keep a profound silence with regard to the Christian fables, which are canonized by their antiquity and the credulity of absurd and insipid people.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Letters of Voltaire and Frederick the Great (New York: Brentano's, 1927), trans. Richard Aldington , letter 37 from Frederick to Voltaire (June 1738)

text: A single Voltaire will do more honor to France than a thousand pedants, a thousand false wits, a thousand great men of inferior order.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Letters of Voltaire and Frederick the Great (New York: Brentano's, 1927), trans. Richard Aldington, letter 48 from Frederick to Voltaire ( 1740-01-06 )

text: Truth to tell, treaties are only oaths of deception and faithlessness. The jurisprudence of sovereigns is customarily the law of the strongest.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Preface to “Histoire de mon temps”, Works (1743), quoted in W. W. Coole (ed.), Thus Spake Germany (London: George Routledge & Sons, 1941), p. 82

text: A prince's oath binds not just himself, but nations; thus, it is better he break his contract than his people perish.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Historie vom Jahre 1746 , quoted in W. W. Coole (ed.), Thus Spake Germany (London: George Routledge & Sons, 1941), p. 82

text: It has been said by a certain general, that the first object in the establishment of an army ought to be making provision for the belly, that being the basis and foundation of all operations.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Military Instructions (1747), Article II: Of the Subsistence of Troops, and of Provisions

text: With all the world's algebra, one is often but a fool knowing nothing else. All that is neither useful nor agreeable is worthless; I see in these calculations only scientific extravagance.
author: Frederick the Great
source: [A] man with all the algebra in the world is often only an ass when he knows nothing else. Perhaps in ten years society may derive advantage from the curves which these visionary algebraists will have laboriously squared. I congratulate posterity beforehand. But to tell you the truth I see nothing but a scientific extravagance in all these calculations . That which is neither useful nor agreeable is worthless. And as for useful things, they have all been discovered; and to those which are agreeable, I hope that good taste will not admit algebra among them.

text: Only sneaky people and impostors can oppose the progress of sciences and can discredit them, because they are the only ones to whom the sciences do harm.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Unknown

text: I know the value of tranquility, but I will not buy it with baseness; duty to country demands our blood and repose.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Letters of Voltaire and Frederick the Great (New York: Brentano's, 1927), trans. Richard Aldington, letter 141 from Frederick to Voltaire ( 1759-07-02 )

text: Russia is Prussia's most dangerous neighbor; cultivate their friendship, for their power can ruin us, and their poverty prevents repayment.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Histoire de mon temps (1763-1764), quoted in W. W. Coole (ed.), Thus Spake Germany (London: George Routledge & Sons, 1941), p. 225

text: Neither antiquity nor any other nation has imagined a more atrocious and blasphemous absurdity than that of eating God . — This is how Christians treat the autocrat of the universe .
author: Frederick the Great
source: Letters of Voltaire and Frederick the Great (New York: Brentano's, 1927), trans. Richard Aldington, letter 215 from Frederick to Voltaire ( 1776-03-19 )

text: The King finds coffee disgusting; his ancestors and soldiers thrived on beer, winning battles, and he doubts coffee-drinkers can endure war.
author: Frederick the Great
source: 1777; quoted by Bert L. Vallée, Alcohol in the Western World , Scientific American, Vol. 278, No. 6 (June), 1998, pp. 80-85

text: As to your Newton , I confess I do not understand his void and his gravity ; I admit he has demonstrated the movement of the heavenly bodies with more exactitude than his forerunners; but you will admit it is an absurdity to maintain the existence of Nothing .
author: Frederick the Great
source: Letters of Voltaire and Frederick the Great (New York: Brentano's, 1927), trans. Richard Aldington, letter 221 from Frederick to Voltaire ( 1777-11-25 )

text: Euler calculated my fountain, yet the geometrically perfect mill failed to raise a drop. Vanity of vanities! Vanity of geometry!
author: Frederick the Great
source: I wanted to have a water jet in my garden: Euler calculated the force of the wheels necessary to raise the water to a reservoir, from where it should fall back through channels, finally spurting out in Sans Souci . My mill was carried out geometrically and could not raise a mouthful of water closer than fifty paces from the reservoir. Vanity of vanities! Vanity of geometry!

text: I shall not survive this cruel misfortune. The consequences will be worse than defeat itself. I have no resources left, and, to speak quite frankly I believe everything is lost. I shall not outlive the downfall of my country. Farewell, forever!
author: Frederick the Great
source: Holmes, Richard; John Pimlott (1999). The Hutchinson Atlas of Battle Plans: Before and After . Taylor & Francis. ISBN 9781579582036 .

text: Machiavelli's *The Prince* corrupted policy and destroyed healthy morals, making it one of the most dangerous works, especially for princes.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Foreword

text: Justice is the sovereign's main responsibility, prioritizing the people's interest; the sovereign is not a master, but the first servant.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Ch. 1 : What A Strong Prince Really Is, And How One Can Reach That Point

text: It is a fact that princes who try to raise other princes with violence, end up destroying themselves.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Ch. 3 : Mixed Principalities

text: "It is enough", this malicious man tells us, "to extinguish the line of the defeated prince." Can one read this without quivering in horror and indignation?
author: Frederick the Great
source: Ch. 3 : Mixed Principalities

text: But France 's powerful armies , and a very large number of fortresses, ensure that the French Sovereign will possess the throne forever , and they do not have anything to fear now concerning internal wars or their neighbors invading France .
author: Frederick the Great
source: Chapter IV

text: From marshy islands, Holland's industrious people built a powerful, rich republic, shaking off domination, extending trade globally, and maintaining a formidable military.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Ch. 5 : How It Is Necessary To Control The Cities, Or The Principalities, Which Are Controlled By Their Own Laws Before They Were Conquered

text: We foolishly conquer as if time were endless, only to find our lives pass quickly, often working for unworthy successors.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Ch. 5 : How It Is Necessary To Control The Cities, Or The Principalities, Which Are Controlled By Their Own Laws Before They Were Conquered

text: If men lacked passions, Machiavelli's attempt to instill them would be forgivable; but unchecked passions are destruction.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Ch. 6 : New States That The Prince Acquires By His Valor And His Own Weapons

text: Hero's policy of demolishing loyal friends and soldiers for new ones is bad; it is more prudent to trust tested loyalty, for the unknown are unsafe.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Ch. 6 : New States That The Prince Acquires By His Valor And His Own Weapons

text: Cesare Borgia committed countless cruelties, yet Machiavelli held him as an example for those fortune favors.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Chapter VII

text: To ensure justice on earth, people must know virtue, for it leads to unity, not war; crime brings only destruction, even to the criminal.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Chapter VIII

text: Just as people are born and die, republics are formed, flower, and perish by a citizen's audacity or enemy weapons; all empires have their time.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Chapter IX

text: A life of indolence, however long, is no life at all. True living is measured by meaningful action.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Chapter XIV

text: Like a long boat which follows in the wake of the warship to which it is tied.
author: Frederick the Great
source: On the decline of the Dutch Republic subject to British power

text: If my soldiers began to think, not one would remain in the ranks.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Attributed in J.A. Houlding, Fit for Service: The Training of the British Army, 1715-1795 (Oxford, 1981)

text: "My people and I," he said, "have come to an agreement which satisfies us both. They are to say what they please, and I am to do what I please."
author: Frederick the Great
source: Attributed by Thomas Babington Macaulay , Life of Frederick the Great (1882), pg 48.

text: Diplomacy without arms is like music without instruments.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Attributed in The Times : "Diplomacy without Arms", 1 February 2010.

text: I begin by taking. I shall find scholars later to demonstrate my perfect right.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Unknown

text: An army, like a serpent, goes upon its belly.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Attributed in Thomas Carlyle , “History of Friedrich the Second, Called Frederick the Great", (1858).

text: Christianity is an old metaphysical fiction, stuffed with fables, contradictions, and absurdities.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Unknown

text: If I had a province to punish, I would let it be governed by philosophers.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Unknown

text: Frederick was an acquired taste: repulsive at first, then addictive, inspiring a sentiment perhaps stronger than love.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Sebastian Haffner , The Rise and Fall of Prussia (1980), p. 32

text: Frederick the Great was elevated to a national divinity, his example becoming the supreme national dogma.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Frederick Hertz , Nationalgeist und Politik , Vol. I (Zuerich, 1937), p. 450, quoted in Lord Vansittart, ‘Preface’, W. M. Knight-Patterson, Germany from Defeat to Conquest, 1913–1933 (1945), p. 9

text: There has never been any prominent man who despised humanity so bitterly and unaffectedly as Frederick despised it.
author: Frederick the Great
source: John Morley , Voltaire (1886), pp. 166–168

text: He turns upon his own axis like the sun, shining in his own light, a colossus among kings.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Friedrich Karl von Moser , Der Herr und der Diener, geschildert mit patriotischer Freiheit (1759), pp. 19–20, quoted in Gerhard Ritter, The Sword and the Sceptre: The Problem of Militarism in Germany, Volume I: The Prussian Tradition, 1740–1890 (1972), p. 36

text: What, in fine, did Frederick bequeath to Prussia? Well, he bequeathed his name and fame as a great conqueror. He became in a secular sense the Patron Saint of Germany.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Lord Rosebery , 'Introduction', in Henri de Catt, Frederick the Great: The Memoirs of His Reader Henri de Catt (1758–1760). Vol. I , translated by F. S. Flint (1916), p. xxxvi

text: We have just sung a Prussian national hymn which celebrates Frederick the Great. What renders that sovereign so present to our memory is not our monarchical faith, but he is for us the personification of the ancient Prussian spirit.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Gustav Stresemann , quoted in W. W. Coole (ed.), Thus Spake Germany (1941), p. 39

text: Frederick the Great embodies the ancient Prussian spirit, the categorical imperative of duty, and the State's supremacy.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Gustav Stresemann , quoted in W. M. Knight-Patterson, Germany from Defeat to Conquest, 1913–1933 (1945), p. 369

text: Frederick the Great asked for proof of God; his physician replied, 'Your Majesty, the Jews!'—a tiny nation that has maintained itself through history.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Dogmatics in Outline (1949) as translated by Tr. G. T. Thomson ISBN 006130056X , quoted in "Word Alive! An Introduction to the Christian Faith" by John Schwarz (1995)

text: Madam, don't bring up your sons to detest the United States government . Recollect that we form one country now. Abandon all these local animosities, and make your sons Americans.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Advice to a Confederate widow who expressed animosity towards the northern U.S. after the end of the American Civil War, as quoted in The Life and Campaigns of General Lee (1875) by Edward Lee Childe, p. 331. Also quoted in "Will Confederate Heritage Advocates Take Robert E. Lee’s Advice?" (July 2014), by Brooks D. Simpson, Crossroads , WordPress. This quote is sometimes paraphrased as: "Madam, do not train up your children in hostility to the government of the United States. Remember, we are all one country now. Dismiss from your mind all sectional feeling, and bring them up to be Americans."

text: We must forgive our enemies. I can truly say that not a day has passed since the war began that I have not prayed for them.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: As quoted in A Life of General Robert E. Lee (1871), by John Esten Cooke

text: I cannot consent to place in the control of others one who cannot control himself.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Comment regarding officers who became inebriated, as quoted in Personal Reminiscences, Anecdotes, and Letters of Gen. Robert E. Lee (1874) by John William Jones, p. 170

text: Sir, if you ever presume again to speak disrespectfully of General Grant in my presence, either you or I will sever his connection with this university .
author: Robert E. Lee
source: After one of the faculty at Washington College in Virginia (now Washington & Lee University) had spoken insultingly of Ulysses S. Grant , as quoted in Lee the American (1912) by Gamaliel Bradford, p. 226

text: A true man of honor feels humbled himself when he cannot help humbling others.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: "Definition of a Gentleman" , a memorandum found in his papers after his death, as quoted in Lee the American (1912) by Gamaliel Bradford, p. 233

text: But I have never cherished toward them bitter or vindictive feelings, and have never seen the day when I did not pray for them.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: As quoted in The American Soul: An Appreciation of the Four Greatest Americans and their Lessons for Present Americans (1920) by Charles Sherwood Farriss, p. 63

text: Obedience to lawful authority is the foundation of manly character.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: As quoted in General Robert E. Lee After Appomattox (1922), by Franklin Lafayette Riley, p. 18

text: I notice, however, that my mistakes are never told me until it is too late, and you, and all my officers, know that I am always ready and anxious to have their suggestions.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Remark to General Henry Heth , as quoted in R. E. Lee : A Biography , Vol. 3 (1935) by Douglas Southall Freeman

text: I should NOT be trading on the blood of my men.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: On refusing requests to write his memoirs, as quoted in Gentlemen of Virginia (1961) page 188 by Marshall William Fishwick; also cited as possibly apocryphal in The Oxford Dictionary of Quotations (2004) edited by Elizabeth M. Knowles

text: The education of a man is never completed until he dies.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: As quoted in Peter's Quotations: Ideas for Our Time (1977) by Laurence J. Peter, p. 175

text: Above all do not appear to others what you are not.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: As quoted in Extraordinary Lives: The Art and Craft of American Biography (1986) by Robert A. Caro and William Knowlton Zinsser. Also quoted in Truman by David McCullough (1992), p. 44, New York: Simon & Schuster.-

text: I think it is the duty of every citizen, in the present condition of the Country, to do all in his power to aid in the restoration of peace and harmony.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to trustees, as quoted in "Honoring Lee Anew" (15 July 2014), by David Cox, A Magazine of Student Thought and Opinion

text: All should unite in honest efforts to obliterate the effects of the war and restore the blessing of peace.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to Governor Letcher

text: The Abolitionist ... must see that he has neither the right or power of operating except by moral means and suasion.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Speech in the Senate (3 March 1854); Quoted in Douglas Southall Freeman (2008) Lee, p. 93

text: Mr. Blair, I look upon secession as anarchy . If I owned the four millions of slaves in the South, I would sacrifice them all to the Union ; but how can I draw my sword upon Virginia, my native State?
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Life and Campaigns of General Robert E. Lee (1866) page 30. Responding to Francis Preston Blair after he relayed an offer to make Lee major-general to command the defense of Washington D.C.

text: You see what a poor sinner I am, and how unworthy to possess what was given me; for that reason, it has been taken away.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to his daughter after losing Arlington (25 December 1861)

text: Still, a Union that can only be maintained by swords and bayonets, and in which strife and civil war are to take the place of brotherly love and kindness, has no charm for me.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to his son , G. W. Custis Lee (23 January 1861).

text: Save for defense of my native state, I never desire again to draw my sword.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to General Winfield Scott (20 April 1861) after turning down an offer by Abraham Lincoln of supreme command of the U.S. Army; as quoted in Personal Reminiscences, Anecdotes, and Letters of Gen. Robert E. Lee (1875) by John William Jones, p. 139

text: It is well that war is so terrible, otherwise we should grow too fond of it.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Comment to James Longstreet , on seeing a Union charge repelled in the Battle of Fredericksburg (13 December 1862)

text: What a cruel thing is war, to separate and destroy families, mar our purest joys, and devastate the beautiful world.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to his wife on Christmas Day, two weeks after the Battle of Fredericksburg (25 December 1862).

text: The only dignified course for me is to surrender, for any other path would bring years of chaos and suffering to the country.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: In response to Brigadier General Edward Porter Alexander (April 1865) after the Battle of Appomattox Court House . Alexander had suggested for Confederate soldiers to disperse, report under orders to their respective governors, and continue fighting the Union. He admitted that he was silenced by Lee's rebuke. "I had not a single word to say in reply. He had answered my suggestion from a plane so far above it that I was ashamed for having made it. " as quoted in Fighting For the Confederacy, pp. 531-33 [ 1 ] and The Methodist Quarterly Review, January 1920 pp. 325-26 [ 2 ]

text: The only question on which we did not agree has been settled, and the Lord has decided against me.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: To Marsena Patrick , as quoted in "Honoring Lee Anew" (15 July 2014), by David Cox, A Magazine of Student Thought and Opinion

text: I solemnly swear to faithfully support, protect, and defend the Constitution, the Union, and all laws regarding the emancipation of slaves.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Amnesty oath to the United States (2 October 1865)

text: When reason failed and war decided, wisdom dictates we acquiesce in the result and candidly recognize the fact.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to former Virginia governor John Letcher (28 August 1865), as quoted in Personal Reminiscences, Anecdotes, and Letters of Gen. Robert E. Lee (1875) by John William Jones, p. 203

text: All should unite to obliterate the effects of war and restore the blessings of peace.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to former Virginia governor John Letcher (28 August 1865), as quoted in Personal Reminiscences, Anecdotes, and Letters of Gen. Robert E. Lee (1875) by John William Jones, p. 203

text: True patriotism sometimes requires of men to act exactly contrary, at one period, to that which it does at another, and the motive which impels them — the desire to do right — is precisely the same.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to General P.G.T. Beauregard (3 October 1865)

text: All should vote for intelligent, honest, and conscientious leaders who will serve the true interests, prosperity, and liberty of all people.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to General James Longstreet (29 October 1867), as quoted in Recollections and Letters of General Robert E. Lee (1924), p. 269.

text: It is wiser not to keep open the sores of war, but to obliterate the marks of civil strife and commit its feelings to oblivion.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter regarding war monuments (1869), as quoted in Personal reminiscences, anecdotes, and letters of gen. Robert E. Lee (1874), by John William Jones , p. 234. Also quoted in "Renounce the battle flag: Don't whitewash history" (26 June 2015), by Petula Dvorak, The Washington Post , Washington, D.C. This quote is also given as: "I think it wisest not to keep open the sores of war, but to follow the example of those nations who endeavored to obliterate the marks of civil strife, and to commit to oblivion the feelings it engendered."

text: True honesty stems from conviction of what is right, not from policy.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: "Memoirs of Robert E. Lee" by A. L. Long (1886)

text: Those who oppose our purposes are not always to be regarded as our enemies. We usually think and act from our immediate surroundings. The better rule is to judge our adversaries from their standpoint, not from ours.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: "Memoirs of Robert E. Lee" by A. L. Long (1886)

text: I have rejoiced that slavery is abolished, believing it great for the South, and would cheerfully have lost all to see this object attained.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Statement to John Leyburn (1 May 1870), as quoted in R. E. Lee: A Biography (1934) by Douglas Southall Freeman.

text: The march of Providence is slow, our desires impatient, and progress immense; yet, it is history that teaches us to hope.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to Lieutenant Colonel Charles Marshall (September 1870)

text: Duty is the sublimest word in our language. Do your duty in all things. You cannot do more. You should never wish to do less.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter purportedly written to his son, G. W. Custis Lee (5 April 1852); published in The New York Sun (26 November 1864). Although the “Duty Letter” was presumed authentic for many decades and included in many biographies of Lee, it was repudiated in December 1864 by “a source entitled to know.” This repudiation was rediscovered by University of Virginia law professor Charles A. Graves who verified that the letter was inconsistent with Lee's biographical facts and letter-writing style. Lee's son also wrote to Graves that he did not recall ever receiving such a letter. “The Forged Letter of General Robert E. Lee”, Proceedings of the 26th annual meeting of the Virginia State Bar Association 17:176 (1914)

text: Had I foreseen these results of subjugation, I would have preferred to die at Appomattox with my brave men, my sword in my right hand.
author: Robert E. Lee
