source: There is a god within us. It is when he stirs us that our bosom warms ; it is his impulse that sows the seeds of inspiration. VI, lines 5-6; translation by Sir James George Frazer

text: Those who deserve punishment bear it with a calm mind.
author: Ovid
source: They bear punishment with equanimity who have earned it. Book II, vii, 12

text: What is allowed feels ungrateful; what is forbidden burns more fiercely.
author: Ovid
source: We take no pleasure in permitted joys. But what's forbidden is more keenly sought. Book II; xix, 3

text: We always strive for the forbidden, and we desire what is denied.
author: Ovid
source: We are ever striving after what is forbidden, and coveting what is denied us.

text: I can live neither without you nor with you.
author: Ovid
source: So I can't live either without you or with you.

text: They come to see, and they come to be seen.
author: Ovid
source: They come to see; they come that they themselves may be seen. Book I, line 99 (tr. Henry T. Riley ) Compare: "And for to see, and eek for to be seye", Geoffrey Chaucer , The Canterbury Tales : "The Wife of Bath's Prologue", line 6134

text: At night, flaws lie hidden, every fault forgiven; that hour makes any woman beautiful.
author: Ovid
source: Blemishes are hid by night and every fault forgiven; darkness makes any woman fair. Book I, lines 249–250

text: Jupiter from on high laughs at the perjuries of lovers.
author: Ovid
source: Jupiter from above laughs at lovers' perjuries . Book I, line 633

text: It is expedient that there be gods; and as it is expedient, let us believe they exist.
author: Ovid
source: It is convenient that there be gods, and, as it is convenient, let us believe that there are. Book I, line 637

text: No law is more just than for the architects of death to perish by their own art.
author: Ovid
source: No fairer law in all the land Than that death-dealers die by what they've planned. Book I, lines 655–656 (tr. Len Krisak )

text: Endure and be firm: soon she will be mild. Compliance bends what force breaks.
author: Ovid
source: If she's cool and unwilling to be wooed, Just take it, don't weaken; in time she'll soften her mood. Bending a bough the right way, gently, makes It easy; use brute force, and it breaks. With swimming rivers it's the same— Go with, not against, the current. Book II, lines 177–182 (tr. James Michie)

text: I am a poet for the poor, for I loved as a poor man; when I could not give gifts, I gave words.
author: Ovid
source: I am the poor man's poet; because I am poor myself and I have known what it is to be in love. Not being able to pay them in presents, I pay my mistresses in poetry. Book II, lines 165–166 (tr. J. Lewis May)

text: While new love wanders, let it gather strength through use: if you nourish it well, it will be firm with time.
author: Ovid
source: Young love is errant, but it needs to get around; The time and practice make it strong and sound. That bull you fear, you petted when it wasn't big; What now you sleep beneath was once a twig. That little stream, in gaining waters as it goes, Grows stronger, till at last a river flows. Book II, lines 339–344 (tr. Len Krisak)

text: Give rest: a rested field returns well what was entrusted to it.
author: Ovid
source: Leave her alone. A fallow field soon shows its worth, And rain is best absorbed by arid earth. Book II, line 351 (tr. Len Krisak)

text: One must make use of time: time glides away with swift foot.
author: Ovid
source: Seize Time; his swift foot can't be held. Book III, line 65 (tr. Len Krisak)

text: Our good things flee without help; pluck the flower, which, unless plucked, will fall shamefully by itself.
author: Ovid
source: Our charms depart all on their own, so pluck the bloom. For if you don't, it meets a wasted doom. Book III, lines 79–80 (tr. Len Krisak)

text: Chance prevails everywhere; let your hook always hang where you least expect it, there will be a fish.
author: Ovid
source: Chance is always powerful. Let your hook always be cast; in the pool where you least expect it, there will be fish. Book III, line 425

text: Bright peace suits men; fierce anger suits beasts.
author: Ovid
source: Rage is for beasts, but shining peace for man. Book III, line 502 (tr. Len Krisak)

text: There is a god within us, and commerce with heaven: that spirit comes from ethereal abodes.
author: Ovid
source: We all conceal A god within us, we all deal With heaven direct, from whose high places we derive The inspiration by which we live. Book III, lines 549–550 (tr. James Michie)

text: Still lacking was an animal more sacred than these, and more capable of lofty thought, and one that could dominate all others: Man was born.
author: Ovid
source: A creature of a more exalted kind Was wanting yet, and then was Man designed; Conscious of thought, of more capacious breast, For empire formed, and fit to rule the rest. Book I, 76 (as translated by John Dryden )

text: While other animals look downwards to the earth, he gave to man a lofty countenance, and bade him look up to heaven, and raise his erect face to the stars.
author: Ovid
source: Thus, while the mute creation downward bend Their sight, and to their earthly mother tend, Man looks aloft, and with erected eyes Beholds his own hereditary skies. Book I, 84 (as translated by John Dryden)

text: Since his years forbid him to fight, he battles by speaking, assailing and cursing wicked arms.
author: Ovid
source: He, because years restrained him from warring, fought by speaking; he attacked and cursed the criminal weapons. Book V, 101-2

text: The use of waters is common. Nature made neither the sun, nor the air, nor the flowing waters private property.
author: Ovid
source: Water belongs to us all. Nature did not make the sun one person's property, nor air, nor water, cool and clear. Book VI, 349-351; translation by Michael Simpson

text: I see the better, and approve it, but I follow the worse.
author: Ovid
source: I see better things, and approve, but I follow worse. Book VII, 20

text: Fly along the middle path: too low, water weighs; too high, fire scorches. Fly between the two.
author: Ovid
source: My son , I caution you to keep The middle way, for if your pinions dip Too low the waters may impede your flight; And if they soar too high the sun may scorch them. Fly midway. Book VIII, lines 203–206; translation by Brooks More

text: He is indeed beautiful, though to envious eyes.
author: Ovid
source: He is beautiful even to enemy eyes. Book IX, 476

text: What is my fault then? Unless to have played can be called a fault, unless to have loved can also be called a fault.
author: Ovid
source: What, however, is my fault? Unless to have played can be called a fault, unless it can be called a fault to have loved. Book X, 200-1

text: For lineage and ancestors, and what we ourselves have not done, I scarcely call our own.
author: Ovid
source: For those things which were done either by our fathers, or ancestors, and in which we ourselves had no share, we can scarcely call our own. Book XIII, 140–141

text: Your strength is mindless; my mind guides the future, for as the pilot surpasses the oarsman, and the general the soldier, so do I surpass you.
author: Ovid
source: Your right arm is useful in the battle; but when it comes to thinking you need my guidance. You have force without intelligence; while mine is the care for to-morrow. You are a good fighter; but is I who help Atrides select the time of fighting. Your value is in your body only; mine, in mind. And, as much as he who directs the ship surpasses him who only rows it, as much as the general exceeds the common soldier, so much greater am I than you. For in these bodies of ours the heart is of more value than the hand; all our real living is in that. Book XIII, 361–369; translation by Frank Justus Miller

text: I foolishly asked for as many birthdays as grains of sand, but forgot to ask for eternal youth.
author: Ovid
source: Pointing to a pile of dust, that had collected, I foolishly begged to have as many anniversaries of my birth, as were represented by the dust. But I forgot to ask that the years should be accompanied by youth. Book XIV, lines 136–139; translation by A. S. Kline

text: Spare your bodies from unholy feasts! The generous earth offers gentle sustenance and banquets without slaughter.
author: Ovid
source: O mortals, from your fellows' blood abstain, Nor taint your bodies with a food profane: While corn, and pulse by Nature are bestow'd, And planted orchards bend their willing load; While labour'd gardens wholesom herbs produce, And teeming vines afford their gen'rous juice; Nor tardier fruits of cruder kind are lost, But tam'd with fire, or mellow'd by the frost; While kine to pails distended udders bring, And bees their hony redolent of Spring; While Earth not only can your needs supply, But, lavish of her store, provides for luxury; A guiltless feast administers with ease, And without blood is prodigal to please. Book XV , 75–82 (from Wikisource); on vegetarianism , as the following quote

text: What a crime, for flesh to be buried in flesh, for one living creature to live by the death of another, when Earth provides such abundance!
author: Ovid
source: O impious use! to Nature's laws oppos'd, Where bowels are in other bowels clos'd: Where fatten'd by their fellow's fat, they thrive; Maintain'd by murder, and by death they live. 'Tis then for nought, that Mother Earth provides The stores of all she shows, and all she hides, If men with fleshy morsels must be fed, And chaw with bloody teeth the breathing bread: What else is this, but to devour our guests, And barb'rously renew Cyclopean feasts! We, by destroying life, our life sustain; And gorge th' ungodly maw with meats obscene. Book XV , 88–95 (from Wikisource)

text: No form remains the same; nature, the renovator, ever creates new shapes from old. Nothing perishes, but changes and renews its face: to be born is to begin to be other, to die is to cease to be that same thing.
author: Ovid
source: No species remains constant: that great renovator of matter Nature, endlessly fashions new forms from old: there’s nothing in the whole universe that perishes, believe me; rather it renews and varies its substance. What we describe as birth is no more than incipient change from a prior state, while dying is merely to quit it. Though the parts may be transported hither and thither, the sum of all matter is constant. Book XV, 252–258 (as translated by Peter Green )

text: Songs flow from a tranquil soul.
author: Ovid
source: Poetry comes fine-spun from a mind at ease. I, i, 39

text: While fortune smiles, you'll count many friends; when clouds gather, you'll be alone.
author: Ovid
source: So long as you are secure you will count many friends; if your life becomes clouded you will be alone. I, ix, 5

text: Ants never seek empty granaries; no friend will seek lost wealth.
author: Ovid
source: Ants never head for an empty granary: no friends gather round when your wealth is gone. I, ix, 9-10; translation by A.S. Kline

text: Believe me, he who lived hidden, lived well; each should remain within their own fortune.
author: Ovid
source: Well doth he live who lives retired, and keeps His wants within the limit of his means.

text: The greater a person, the more placable their anger; a noble mind is easily moved, for a magnanimous lion is satisfied when its foe is prostrate, unlike lesser beasts that attack the dying.
author: Ovid
source: The greater a man is, the more can his wrath be appeased; a noble spirit is capable of kindly impulses. For the noble lion 'tis enough to have overthrown his enemy; the fight is at an end when his foe is fallen. But the wolf, the ignoble bears harry the dying and so with every beast of less nobility. At Troy what have we mightier than brave Achilles ? But the tears of the aged Dardanian he could not endure. III, v, 33; translation by Arthur Leslie Wheeler Note: "the aged Dardanian" here refers to Priam

text: You say I live, but in a way I wish I did not.
author: Ovid
source: Say that I live, but in such wise that I would not live. III, vii, 7; translation by Arthur Leslie Wheeler

text: It is difficult, I confess, but virtue strives for the heights, and the grace of such merit will be greater.
author: Ovid
source: ' Tis hard, I admit, yet virtue aims at what is hard , and gratitude for such a service will be all the greater. II, ii, 111-112; translation by Arthur Leslie Wheeler
source_section: Epistulae ex Ponto (Letters From the Black Sea)

text: Shameful to say, but if we confess the truth, the crowd values friendships for their utility.
author: Ovid
source: Shameful it is to say, yet the common herd, if only we admit the truth, value friendships by their profit. II, iii, 7-8; translation by Arthur Leslie Wheeler
source_section: Epistulae ex Ponto (Letters From the Black Sea)

text: Rarely will you find one who deems virtue its own reward; nothing is dear unless it is profitable.
author: Ovid
source: Nor can one easily find among many thousands a single man who considers virtue its own reward. The very glory of a good deed, if it lacks reward, affects them not; unrewarded uprightness brings them regret. Nothing but profit is prized. II, iii, 11-15; translation by Arthur Leslie Wheeler. Variant translation of gratis paenitet esse probum , in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 15th ed. (1980), p. 114: "It is annoying to be honest to no purpose."
source_section: Epistulae ex Ponto (Letters From the Black Sea)

text: To have faithfully learned the liberal arts softens manners and prevents savagery.
author: Ovid
source: Note too that a faithful study of the liberal arts humanizes character and permits it not to be cruel. II, ix, 47
source_section: Epistulae ex Ponto (Letters From the Black Sea)

text: Though strength may fail, the will is still to be praised.
author: Ovid
source: Though strength be lacking, yet the will is to be praised. III, iv, 79
source_section: Epistulae ex Ponto (Letters From the Black Sea)

text: Joyful, I sang of joy; sorrowful, I sing of sorrow.
author: Ovid
source: Gay was oft my song when I was gay, sad it is now that I am sad. III, ix, 35; translation by Arthur Leslie Wheeler
source_section: Epistulae ex Ponto (Letters From the Black Sea)

text: Yesterday has not yet been born; it has not yet really existed. I want Ovid, Pushkin, and Catullus to live once more, not merely as historical figures.
author: Ovid
source: Osip Mandelstam THE WORD AND CULTURE translated into English in The complete critical prose (1997)

text: Who now journeys on that dark path, from which no one, they say, returns.
author: Catullus
source: Now he goes along the dark road, thither whence they say no one returns.

text: Suns can set and rise again; but when our brief light sets, one perpetual night awaits.
author: Catullus
source: Let us live, my Lesbia, and love, and value at one farthing all the talk of crabbed old men. Suns may set and rise again. For us, when the short light has once set, remains to be slept the sleep of one unbroken night.

text: He himself does not know who he is, whether he exists or not.
author: Catullus
source: What he himself is, whether he is or is not, he does not know so much as this.

text: What is more blessed than cares released, when the mind lays down its burden and we rest in the longed-for bed of home?
author: Catullus
source: Ah, what is more blessed than to put cares away, when the mind lays by its burden, and tired with labour of far travel we have come to our own home and rest on the couch we longed for? This it is which alone is worth all these toils.

text: He seems to me equal to a god, even to surpass the gods, who gazes at you and hears your sweet laughter.
author: Catullus
source: He seems to me to be equal to a god, he, if it may be, seems to surpass the very gods, who sitting opposite thee again and again gazes at thee and hears thee sweetly laughing.

text: Leisure has destroyed both kings and blessed cities before.
author: Catullus
source: Idleness ere now has ruined both kings and wealthy cities.

text: As a flower grows secretly in enclosed gardens, unknown to the flock, untouched by any plow, caressed by breezes, strengthened by sun, nourished by rain.
author: Catullus
source: As a flower springs up secretly in a fenced garden, unknown to the cattle, torn up by no plough, which the winds caress, the sun strengthens, the shower draws forth, many boys, many girls, desire it.

text: Let no woman believe a man's sworn words; for while desire burns, they promise all, but once lust is satisfied, they care nothing for perjuries.
author: Catullus
source: Henceforth let no woman believe a man's oath, let none believe that a man's speeches can be trustworthy. They, while their mind desires something and longs eagerly to gain it, nothing fear to swear, nothing spare to promise; but as soon as the lust of their greedy mind is satisfied, they fear not then their words, they heed not their perjuries.

text: All things, right and wrong, mixed in evil madness, have turned away from us the just mind of the gods.
author: Catullus
source: All right and wrong, confounded in impious madness, turned from us the righteous will of the gods.

text: What a woman says to an eager lover should be written in wind and swift water.
author: Catullus
source: What a woman says to her ardent lover should be written in wind and running water.

text: Cease to wish to deserve anything good from anyone, or to think that anyone can become pious.
author: Catullus
source: Leave off wishing to deserve any thanks from anyone, or thinking that anyone can ever become grateful.

text: My mind, Lesbia, is so destroyed by your fault, that it can neither wish you well, if you become the best, nor cease to love you, if you do all things.
author: Catullus
source: To this point is my mind reduced by your fault, Lesbia, and has so ruined itself by its own devotion, that now it can neither wish you well though you should become the best of women, nor cease to love you though you do the worst that can be done.

text: It is difficult to suddenly lay aside a long-standing love.
author: Catullus
source: It is difficult suddenly to lay aside a long-standing love.

text: I hate and I love. Why I do this, perhaps you ask. I know not, but I feel it happen and I am tormented.
author: Catullus
source: I hate and love. Why I do so, perhaps you ask. I know not, but I feel it, and I am in torment.

text: And forever, brother, hail and farewell.
author: Catullus
source: Wandering through many countries and over many seas I come, my brother, to these sorrowful obsequies, to present you with the last guerdon of death, and speak, though in vain, to your silent ashes, since fortune has taken your own self away from me—alas, my brother, so cruelly torn from me! Yet now meanwhile take these offerings, which by the custom of our fathers have been handed down—a sorrowful tribute—for a funeral sacrifice; take them, wet with many tears of a brother, and for ever, my brother, hail and farewell!

text: If anything desired and longed for ever happened to one hoping against hope, this is truly pleasing to the soul.
author: Catullus
source: If anything ever happened to any one who eagerly longed and never hoped, that is a true pleasure to the mind.

text: He sees with a single glance, and does not strike a second time.
author: Catullus
source: John William Mackail , Latin Literature (1896), p. 61

text: Yesterday has not yet been born; I want Ovid, Pushkin, and Catullus to live once more.
author: Catullus
source: Osip Mandelstam THE WORD AND CULTURE translated into English in The complete critical prose (1997)

text: He is vindictive, venomous, and full of obscene malice; he is only tender about his brother.
author: Catullus
source: Harold Nicolson , Diaries and Letters , Vol. III: The Later Years, 1945–1962 , pp. 332–333

text: The most hard-edged and intense of the Latin poets.
author: Catullus
source: Ezra Pound , letter to Harriet Monroe (February 1916), in The Selected Letters of Ezra Pound, 1907–1941 (1950), p. 69

text: Tenderest of Roman poets nineteen-hundred years ago.
author: Catullus
source: Alfred, Lord Tennyson , 'Frater Ave atque Vale' (1883), line 6

text: Augustine, Augustine, what do you seek? Do you think you can pour the whole sea into a small cup?
author: Saint Augustine
source: "Augustinus, Augustinus, what are you trying to do? Do you believe to be able to pour the whole sea in a little jar? As quoted in the letter of Augustine to saint Cyril of Jerusalem related to the treaty titled On the Trinity

text: Do not go outside, return into yourself. In the inner man dwells truth. And if you find your nature mutable, transcend even yourself.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Do not go outside yourself, return to yourself: truth dwells in the interiority of man and, if you find that your nature is changeable, transcend yourself too . As quoted in De vera religione , XXXIX, 72

text: Nowhere in the Gospel do we read that the Lord said: 'I am sending you a Paraclete who will teach you about the course of the sun and moon.' For He wanted to make Christians, not mathematicians.
author: Saint Augustine
source: De actis cum Felice Manicheo {AD 404), translated as A Debate with Felix the Manichean , ¶1709, in The Faith of the Early Fathers Vol 3 : St. Augustine to the End of the Patristic Age by W.A. Jurgens, p. 88

text: The superfluities of the rich are the necessaries of the poor. They who possess superfluities, possess the goods of others.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Patrologia Latina , vol. 37, p. 1922

text: Love men, hate vices.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Love the sinner and hate the sin . Opera Omnia , Vol II. Col. 962, letter 211

text: God judged it better to bring good out of evil than to suffer no evil to exist.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Enchiridion ( c. 420 ), Ch. 27

text: The confession of evil works is the first beginning of good works.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Tractates on the Gospel of John ; tractate XII on John 3:6-21, § 13

text: Do not seek to understand in order to believe, but believe in order to understand.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Therefore do not seek to understand in order to believe , but believe that thou mayest understand. Tractates on the Gospel of John ; tractate XXIX on John 7:14-18, §6 A Select Library of the Nicene And Post-Nicene Fathers of the Christian Church Volume VII by St. Augustine, chapter VII (1888) as translated by Philip Schaff . Compare: Anselm of Canterbury : "Nor do I seek to understand that I may believe, but I believe that I may understand".

text: Like light, the Sacrament's spiritual power is received pure and remains unstained, even through the defiled.
author: Saint Augustine
source: For the spiritual power of a sacrament is like light in this way: it is both received pure by those to be enlightened, and if it passes through the impure it is not defiled. Tractates on the Gospel of John ; tractate V on John 1:33, §15; translation by R. Willems

text: The Sermon on the Mount offers a perfect standard for Christian life, guiding us like a house built upon a rock.
author: Saint Augustine
source: On the Sermon on the Mount , as translated by William Findlay (1888), Book I, Ch. 1

text: The poor in spirit are the humble and God-fearing, for pride is the beginning of all sin.
author: Saint Augustine
source: On the Sermon on the Mount , as translated by William Findlay (1888), Book I, Ch. 1

text: If thou shouldst say, 'It is enough, I have reached perfection ,' all is lost. For it is the function of perfection to make one know one's imperfection.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Quoted by Aldous Huxley , in The Perennial Philosophy (1945)

text: Will is to grace as the horse is to the rider.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Unknown

text: If there is something more excellent than the truth , then that is God ; if not, then truth itself is God.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Unknown

text: The righteous endured the unrighteous, mingling in one temple but not in heart, maintaining unity despite all.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Early Christian Latin Poets , 2000, Carolinne White, Routledge, London, ISBN 0415187826 ISBN 9780415187824 p. 55.
source_section: Psalmus Contra Partem Donati - Psalm Against the Donatists (c. 393)

text: That is the rock, which the proud gates of hell do not overcome.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Come, brethren, if you wish to be engrafted in the vine. It grieves us to see you thus lie cut off. Number the priests in the very chair of Peter, And see in that order of fathers who succeeded the other . This is the rock which the proud gates of hell overcome not. Publications of the Catholic Truth Society (1895), Catholic Truth Society, London, vol. 24, p. 42.
source_section: Psalmus Contra Partem Donati - Psalm Against the Donatists (c. 393)

text: You have made us for yourself, and our heart is restless until it rests in you.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Man desires to praise thee, for he is a part of thy creation; he bears his mortality about with him and carries the evidence of his sin and the proof that thou dost resist the proud. Still he desires to praise thee, this man who is only a small part of thy creation. Thou hast prompted him, that he should delight to praise thee, for thou hast made us for thyself and restless is our heart until it comes to rest in thee.

text: The weakness of little children's limbs is innocent, not their souls .
author: Saint Augustine

text: I had no motive for my wickedness except wickedness itself; I loved my fall, not the object for which I had fallen, but my fall itself.
author: Saint Augustine
source: II, 4

text: I did not yet love, yet I loved to love... I sought what I might love, loving to love.
author: Saint Augustine
source: I was not yet in love , yet I loved to love...I sought what I might love, in love with loving.

text: The sun and moon were but dishes, serving me, who hungered for you.
author: Saint Augustine
source: And these were the dishes wherein to me, hunger-starven for thee, they served up the sun and the moon.

text: Truth is not found in eloquence or rudeness of speech, but in wisdom itself, like wholesome food served in any dish.
author: Saint Augustine

text: I loved to excuse my soul by blaming another nature, but it was I who sinned, and my denial made the impiety incurable.
author: Saint Augustine
source: A. Outler, trans. (Dover: 2002), Book 5, Chapter 10, p. 77

text: The Catholic faith could be maintained without presumption, especially after allegorical interpretation of the Old Testament saved me from spiritual death.
author: Saint Augustine
source: A. Outler, trans. (Dover: 2002), Book 5, Chapter 14, p. 81.

text: I read there that God the Word was born "not of flesh nor of blood, nor of the will of man, nor the will of the flesh, but of God." But, that "the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us" — I found this nowhere there.
author: Saint Augustine
source: VII, 9

text: Grant me chastity and continence, but not yet.
author: Saint Augustine
source: As a youth I prayed, " Give me chastity and continence, but not right now. "

text: I heard a voice chanting, 'Take up and read, take up and read.' I opened the book, and as I read, a light of certainty flooded my heart, banishing all doubt.
author: Saint Augustine
source: I was saying these things and weeping in the most bitter contrition of my heart, when suddenly I heard the voice of a boy or a girl I know not which — coming from the neighboring house, chanting over and over again, " Take up and read; take up and read ." Immediately I ceased weeping and began most earnestly to think whether it was usual for children in some kind of game to sing such a song, but I could not remember ever having heard the like. So, damming the torrent of my tears, I got to my feet, for I could not but think that this was a divine command to open the Bible and read the first passage I should light upon. For I had heard how Anthony, accidentally coming into church while the gospel was being read, received the admonition as if what was read had been addressed to him: "Go and sell what you have and give it to the poor, and you shall have treasure in heaven; and come and follow me" (Matt. 19:21). By such an oracle he was forthwith converted to thee. So I quickly returned to the bench where Alypius was sitting, for there I had put down the apostle’s book when I had left there. I snatched it up, opened it, and in silence read the paragraph on which my eyes first fell: "Not in rioting and drunkenness, not in chambering and wantonness, not in strife and envying, but put on the Lord Jesus Christ , and make no provision for the flesh to fulfill the lusts thereof" (Rom. 13:13). I wanted to read no further, nor did I need to. For instantly, as the sentence ended, there was infused in my heart something like the light of full certainty and all the gloom of doubt vanished away.

text: I asked the whole frame of earth about my God, and it answered, 'I am not he, but he made me.'
author: Saint Augustine

text: Too late have I loved you, O Beauty ever ancient, ever new, too late have I loved you! And behold, you were within, and I outside, and there I sought you.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Late have I loved you, O Beauty ever ancient and ever new! Late have I loved you! And, behold, you were within me, and I out of myself, and there I searched for you. X, 27, as translated in Theology and Discovery: Essays in honor of Karl Rahner, S.J. (1980) edited by William J. Kelly

text: I love a certain kind of light, and sound, and fragrance, and food, and embracement in loving my God, who is the light, sound, fragrance, food, and embracement of my inner man.
author: Saint Augustine

text: Grant what You command, and command what You will.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Give what you command, and command what you will. You impose continency on us.

text: People travel to wonder at mountains, waves, rivers, oceans, and stars; and they pass by themselves without wondering.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Variant: Men go abroad to admire the heights of mountains, the mighty billows of the sea, the broad tides of rivers, the compass of the ocean, and the circuits of the stars, and pass themselves by.

text: From the malady of curiosity, men search out the secret powers of nature, which to know profits not, and wherein they desire nothing but to know.
author: Saint Augustine

text: To the question, 'What was God doing before He made heaven and earth?' I would rather answer, 'I do not know what I do not know,' than give a false reply.
author: Saint Augustine
source: How, then, shall I respond to him who asks, “What was God doing before he made heaven and earth?” I do not answer, as a certain one is reported to have done facetiously (shrugging off the force of the question). “He was preparing hell,” he said, “for those who pry too deep.” It is one thing to see the answer; it is another to laugh at the questioner--and for myself I do not answer these things thus. More willingly would I have answered, “I do not know what I do not know,” than cause one who asked a deep question to be ridiculed--and by such tactics gain praise for a worthless answer.

text: What then is time? If no one asks me, I know; if I wish to explain it to one who asks, I know not.
author: Saint Augustine
source: What then is time ? If no one asks me, I know what it is. If I wish to explain it to him who asks, I do not know.

text: You called and shattered my deafness; You were radiant and put to flight my blindness; You were fragrant, and I pant after You; I tasted You, and now hunger and thirst; You touched me, and I am set on fire for Your peace.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Unknown

text: God, grant men to see in small things the common notions of small and great things.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Translation: God, grant us men to see in a small thing principles which are common things both small and great.

text: Wonderful is the depth of Your oracles, whose surface invites the little ones; and yet wonderful is the depth, O my God, wonderful is the depth.
author: Saint Augustine
source: XII, 14

text: The good man is neither uplifted by the good things of time, nor broken by its ills; but the wicked man, corrupted by this world’s happiness, feels punished by its unhappiness.
author: Saint Augustine

text: So material a difference does it make, not what ills are suffered, but what kind of man suffers them. For, stirred up with the same movement, mud exhales a horrible stench, and ointment emits a fragrant odor.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Variant translations:

text: Thus, in this universal catastrophe, the sufferings of Christians have tended to their moral improvement, because they viewed them with eyes of faith.
author: Saint Augustine

text: The very lust for domination, more rampant in the Roman people than other vices, once it conquered in a few powerful men, crushed the rest under the yoke of servitude.
author: Saint Augustine
source: The lust for power, which of all human vices was found in its most concentrated form in the Roman people as a whole, first established its victory in a few powerful individuals, and then crushed the rest of an exhausted country beneath the yoke of slavery. For when can that lust for power in arrogant hearts come to rest until, after passing from one office to another, it arrives at sovereignty? Now there would be no occasion for this continuous progress if ambition were not all-powerful; and the essential context for ambition is a people corrupted by greed and sensuality. as translated by H. Bettenson (1972), Book 1, Chapter 31, p. 42

text: The Heavenly City outshines Rome, beyond comparison. There, instead of victory, is truth; instead of high rank, holiness; instead of peace, felicity; instead of life, eternity.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Book II, Chapter 29

text: Therefore the good man, although he is a slave, is free; but the bad man, even if he reigns, is a slave, not of one man, but of as many masters as he has vices.
author: Saint Augustine
source: IV, 3

text: Without justice, what are kingdoms but great robberies? A pirate, asked by Alexander why he infested the sea, replied: 'Because I do it with a small boat, I am called a robber; because you do it with a large fleet, you are called an emperor.'
author: Saint Augustine
source: Justice being taken away, then, what are kingdoms but great robberies? For what are robberies themselves, but little kingdoms? The band itself is made up of men; it is ruled by the authority of a prince, it is knit together by the pact of the confederacy; the booty is divided by the law agreed on. If, by the admittance of abandoned men, this evil increases to such a degree that it holds places, fixes abodes, takes possession of cities, and subdues peoples, it assumes the more plainly the name of a kingdom, because the reality is now manifestly conferred on it, not by the removal of covetousness, but by the addition of impunity. Indeed, that was an apt and true reply which was given to Alexander the Great by a pirate who had been seized. For when that king had asked the man what he meant by keeping hostile possession of the sea, he answered with bold pride, “What thou meanest by seizing the whole earth; but because I do it with a petty ship, I am called a robber, whilst thou who dost it with a great fleet art styled emperor.” IV, 4

text: Our ultimate good is to cling to God, for in His embrace, the soul is filled with true virtues. This is the end of all our striving.
author: Saint Augustine
source: We direct our course towards him with love, so that in reaching him we may find our rest, and attain our happiness because we have achieved our fulfillment in him. For our Good, that Final Good about which the philosophers dispute, is nothing else but to cleave to him whose spiritual embrace, if one may so express it, fills the intellectual soul and makes it fertile with true virtues. We are commanded to love this Good with all our heart, with all our soul, with all our strength; and to this Good we must be led by those who love us, and to it we must lead those whom we love. Thus are fulfilled those two commands on which 'all the Law and the prophets depend': 'Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, with all thy soul, and with all thy mind', and 'Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself'. For in order that a man may know how to love himself an end has been established for him to which he is to refer all his action, so that he may attain to bliss. For if a man loves himself, his one wish is to achieve blessedness. Now this end is 'to cling to God'. Thus, if a man knows how to love himself, the commandment to love his neighbour bids him to do all he can to bring his neighbour to love God. (translated by Henry Bettenson) X, 3

text: Evil has no positive nature; but the loss of good has received the name 'evil.'
author: Saint Augustine
source: XI, 9

text: If I am deceived, I am. For he who is not, cannot be deceived.
author: Saint Augustine
source: XI, 26 , Parts of this passage has been heavily compared with later statements of René Descartes ; in Latin and with a variant translations:

text: Beauty is indeed a good gift of God; but that the good may not think it a great good, God dispenses it even to the wicked.
author: Saint Augustine
source: XV, 22

text: Satan transforms himself into an angel of light. We need God's mercy to discern, lest we befriend demons in disguise, mistaking them for angels.
author: Saint Augustine
source: XIX, 9

text: A people is defined by the objects of their love. To know their character, observe what they love; they are superior in proportion to higher interests, inferior to lower.
author: Saint Augustine
source: XIX, 24

text: Christ is the Head, His Church the body, two in one flesh. Those who dissent from Scripture or break the Church's unity are not truly in the Church.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Encyclical letter of Pope Leo XIII on the Unity of the Church , June 29, 1896, ch. 16, Publications of the Catholic Truth Society , 1896, London, Volume 30, p. 41. Alternate translation: The whole Christ is Head and Body. The Head, the only begotten Son of God; and His Body, the Church: the Bridegroom and the Bride, two in one flesh. Whosoever dissent from the Holy Scriptures in respect of the Head, even though they be found in all the places in which the Church is marked out to be, are not in the Church. And again, whosoever agree with the Holy Scriptures concerning the Head, and do not communicate with the unity of the Body, are not in the Church, because they dissent from Christ's own witness concerning Christ's Body, which is the Church. Dr. Pusey , and the Ancient Church (1866), by Thomas W. Allies, Longmans, Green, London, p. 82
source_section: De Unitate Ecclesiae - On the Unity of the Church (c. 401 – 405)

text: We may not assent to bishops' teachings if they contradict canonical Scriptures. Yet, if they maintain unity and charity, God shall reveal truth to them.
author: Saint Augustine
source: The Unity of the Church (1842), Henry Edward Manning , John Murray (pub.), London, p. 52. Comment: “As for contemporary figures, Augustine urged the Donatist faithful not to heed their bishops who were perpetuating the schism. ‘Neither should the Catholic bishops be followed when they are wrong and hold an opinion contrary to the canonical Scriptures of God.’ As Congar explains, each bishop is united to the faith of his Church which in turn is dependent on Scripture. In saying this, Augustine apparently did not take seriously the possibility of some quasi-universal opposition to Scripture among Catholic bishops, but rather envisaged a possible local or regional disruption. Augustinian Studies , 1980, vols. 11-12, Augustinian Institute, Villanova, Pa., Villanova University Press, p. 138.
source_section: De Unitate Ecclesiae - On the Unity of the Church (c. 401 – 405)

text: The light will not shame you, if it shows you your own ugliness, and that ugliness so offends you that you perceive the beauty of the light.
author: Saint Augustine
source: First Homily, as translated by John Burnaby (1955), p. 262
source_section: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John (414)

text: What is love's perfection? To love our enemies, and to love them to the end that they may be our brothers.
author: Saint Augustine
source: First Homily, as translated by John Burnaby (1955), p. 266
source_section: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John (414)

text: In a quarrel for earth, turn not to earth.
author: Saint Augustine
source: First Homily, as translated by John Burnaby (1955), p. 267
source_section: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John (414)

text: Shut out the evil love of the world, that you may be filled with the love of God. You are a vessel that was already full: you must pour away what you have, that you may take in what you have not.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Second Homily, as translated by John Burnaby (1955), p. 274
source_section: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John (414)

text: If a betrothed prefers the ring to the one who made it, is not her heart unfaithful? God has given you all things; therefore, love Him who made them.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Second Homily, as translated by John Burnaby (1955), pp. 275-276
source_section: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John (414)

text: Let each look to his own heart: let him not keep hatred against his brother for any hard word; on account of earthly contention let him not become earth.
author: Saint Augustine
source: First Homily, Paragraph 11, as translated by H. Browne, Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers, First Series , Vol. 7 (1888)
source_section: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John (414)

text: As love grows in you, so too does beauty; for charity itself is the soul's beauty.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Beauty grows in you to the extent that love grows, because charity itself is the soul 's beauty. Ninth Homily, Paragraph 9, as translated by Boniface Ramsey (2008) Augustinian Heritage Institute
source_section: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John (414)

text: The inclination to seek the truth is safer than the presumption which regards unknown things as known.
author: Saint Augustine
source: (Cambridge: 2002), Book 9, Chapter 1, p. 24

text: When I, who conduct this inquiry, love something, then three things are found: I, what I love, and the love itself. … There are, therefore three things: the lover, the beloved and the love.
author: Saint Augustine
source: (Cambridge: 2002), Book 9, Chapter 2, Section 2, p. 26

text: The mind itself, its love [of itself] and its knowledge [of itself] are a kind of trinity.
author: Saint Augustine
source: (Cambridge: 2002), Book 9, Chapter 4, Section 4, p. 27

text: Yes, you see the Trinity if you see charity .
author: Saint Augustine
source: De Trinitate VIII 8,12 .

text: We live in the last days, though the hour of the end remains an impenetrable secret.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Unknown

text: No salvation outside the Church.
author: Saint Augustine
source: There is no salvation outside the church.

text: To divorce and remarry while your first spouse lives is to dwell in perpetual adultery, barring you from grace until the new union is abandoned.
author: Saint Augustine
source: De adulterinis coniugiis , 2, 16, in Bishop Athanasius Schneider , Reaction to Synod Door to communion for divorced & remarried officially kicked open , November 2nd, 2015

text: A good Christian must beware of all impious diviners, even those who speak truth, lest they ensnare the soul in a demonic pact.
author: Saint Augustine
source: II, xvii, 37

text: In obscure matters of faith, do not rush to a rigid interpretation, lest the progress of truth undermine your stance and you fall with it.
author: Saint Augustine
source: I, xviii, 37. Modern translation by J.H. Taylor

text: When science reveals truth, it will not contradict Scripture. When a theory opposes Scripture, it is false. In interpreting sacred texts, if the author's intent is veiled, let faith guide your understanding.
author: Saint Augustine
source: I, xxi, 41. Modern translation by J.H. Taylor

text: It is a disgrace when Christians speak ignorantly of natural science, which unbelievers know by reason and experience, for such folly makes our sacred texts seem false and hinders the salvation of those we seek to reach.
author: Saint Augustine
source: I, xix, 39.

text: Love, and do what thou wilt: for from the root of love, nothing but good can spring.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Tractatus VII, 8

text: God is one, and the Church is a unity; only unity can respond to him. To claim the Church has perished because you are not in it is an impudent assertion; the Church endures, even if you do not.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Exposition 2 of Psalm 108. The unity and perpetuity of the Church against the Donatists .

text: We make a ladder of our vices, if we trample those same vices underfoot.
author: Saint Augustine

text: Faith alone is not enough; even demons believe and tremble. True faith must be joined with works, for it is faith working through love.
author: Saint Augustine

text: God does not love to condemn, but to save; thus, He is patient with the wicked, that He may make good from evil.
author: Saint Augustine
source: For God loves to save and not to condemn ; therefore is he patient with evil, that out of evil good may be brought. 18

text: Do you wish to be great? Begin with the least. To build a mighty edifice, first lay the deep foundation of humility.
author: Saint Augustine
source: You wish to be great , begin from the least. You are thinking to construct some mighty fabric in height; first think of the foundation of humility . And how great soever a mass of building one may wish and design to place above it, the greater the building is to be, the deeper does he dig his foundation. Sermon 19:2 on the New Testament

text: What is anger? The lust for vengeance. What is hatred? Inveterate anger.
author: Saint Augustine
source: What is anger? A lust for revenge. What is hatred? An anger grown old. 58

text: Give to the poor: I ask, I warn, I command, I order.
author: Saint Augustine
source: So give to the poor; I’m begging you, I’m warning you, I’m commanding you, I’m ordering you. 61:13

text: Do not mistake material poverty for spiritual blessedness. God observes your will, not your means. Only the poor in spirit inherit the kingdom of heaven, not those who are merely poor yet greedy or wicked.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Sermon 346A:6 (c. 399 A.D.) "On the Word of God as Leader of the Christians on Their Pilgrimage," Works of Saint Augustine: A Translation for the 21st Century , III/10, Sermons, 341-400, New City Press, Edmund Hill O.P., trans., (1995), ISBN 1565480554 ISBN 9781565480285 , p. 74.

text: The fellow who eggs you on to avenge yourself will rob you of what you were going to say – as we forgive our debtors . When you have forfeited that, all your sins will be held against you; absolutely nothing is forgiven.
author: Saint Augustine

text: The dove loves even when it strikes. The wolf hates even when it flatters.
author: Saint Augustine
source: The dove loves even when it attacks; the wolf hates even when it flatters. 64

text: Bad times, hard times, this is what people keep saying; but let us live well, and times shall be good. We are the times: Such as we are, such are the times.
author: Saint Augustine

text: God became man that man might become God.
author: Saint Augustine
source: God became man so that man might become God.

text: He who created you without you will not justify you without you.
author: Saint Augustine

text: He who created us without our help will not save us without our consent.
author: Saint Augustine
source: St. Augustine, Sermo 169, 11, 13: PL 38, 923 as quoted in Fr. Mitch Pacwa, S. J.. Saved: A Bible Study Guide for Catholics (p. 15). Our Sunday Visitor. Kindle Edition.

text: Charity is the root of all good works.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Charity is the root of all good works . 179A:5:1

text: From the moment I began to serve God, I grew afraid of forswearing myself and applied the brakes to this old habit.
author: Saint Augustine

text: You believe God is one; you do well; the demons also believe, and tremble.
author: Saint Augustine

text: So the Church too, like Mary, enjoys perpetual virginity and uncorrupted fecundity.
author: Saint Augustine

text: Don’t hold yourselves cheap, seeing that the creator of all things and of you estimates your value so high, so dear, that he pours out for you every day the most precious blood of his only-begotten Son.
author: Saint Augustine

text: Nobody should ever doubt that in the washing of rebirth (Titus 3:5) absolutely all sins, from the least to the greatest, are altogether forgiven.
author: Saint Augustine

text: You can live, provided you live; that is, you can live for ever, provided you live a good life.
author: Saint Augustine

text: Worship the God of the martyrs.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Venerate the martyrs, praise, love, proclaim, honor them. But worship the God of the martyrs . 273:9; translation from: The works of Saint Augustine , John E. Rotelle, New City Press, ISBN 1565480600 ISBN 9781565480605 p . 21.

text: An emperor came to Rome, not to his proud temple, but to the humble tomb of the fisherman, seeking what pride could not merit.
author: Saint Augustine
source: In our own times, you see, an emperor came to the city of Rome, where there’s the temple of an emperor, where there’s a fisherman’s tomb . And so that pious and Christian emperor, wishing to beg for health, for salvation from the Lord, did not proceed to the temple of a proud emperor, but to the tomb of a fisherman, where he could imitate that fisherman in humility, so that he, being thus approached, might then obtain something from the Lord, which a haughty emperor would be quite unable to earn. 341:4; English from: Newly Discovered Sermons , 1997, Edmund Hill, tr., John E. Rotelle, ed., New City Press, New York, ISBN 1565481038 ISBN 9781565481039p . p. 286.

text: We can die and sin by ourselves, but we cannot rise by ourselves. Our hope must be in God alone.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Why, being dead, do you rely on yourself? You were able to die of your own accord; you cannot come back to life of your own accord. We were able to sin by ourselves, and we are still able to, nor shall we ever not be able to. Let our hope be in nothing but in God. Let us send up our sighs to him; as for ourselves, let us strive with our wills to earn merit by our prayers. 348A:4 Against Pelagius ; English translation from: Newly Discovered Sermons , 1997, Edmund Hill, John E. Rotelle, New City Press, New York, ISBN 1565481038 , 9781565481039 pp. 311-312. Editor’s comment: “This sounds like a slightly Pelagian remark! But it is presumably intended to reverse what one may call the Pelagian order of things; and see the last few sections of the sermon, 9-15, on the effect of the heresy on prayer.”

text: Do not be disturbed by perverse praise; hostile snares disturb you, but Christ's humility and humanity console you. They preach how much Christ was exalted from you; Christ says how much He descended to you.
author: Saint Augustine
source: So they [the pagans] are going to say, “You tell me that Christ has risen again, and from that you hope for the resurrection of the dead; but Christ was in a position to rise from the dead.” And now he begins to praise Christ, not in order to do him honor, but to make you despair. It is the deadly cunning of the serpent, to turn you away from Christ by praising Christ, to extol deceitfully the one he doesn’t dare to disparage. He exaggerates the sovereign majesty of Christ in order to make him out quite unique , to stop you hoping for anything like what was demonstrated in his rising again. And he seems, apparently, to be all the more religiously respectful of Christ, when he says, “Look at the person who dares compare himself to Christ, so that just because Christ rose again, he can imagine that he's going to rise again too!” Don't let this perverse praise of your emperor disturb you. The insidious tricks of the enemy may disturb you, but the humility and humanity of Christ should console you. This man emphasizes how high above you Christ has been lifted up; Christ, though, says how low he came down to you. Sermon 361 On the Resurrection of the Dead ; 15 How to answer their exaggerated praise of Christ and their disparaging of Christians .

text: For if a thing is not diminished by being shared with others, it is not rightly owned if it is only owned and not shared.
author: Saint Augustine
source: 1:1:1 English Latin

text: We were ensnared by the wisdom of the serpent; we are set free by the foolishness of God.
author: Saint Augustine

text: Since you cannot do good to all, you are to pay special regard to those who, by the accidents of time, or place, or circumstance, are brought into closer connection with you.
author: Saint Augustine
source: 1:28:29 English Latin

text: I have read in Plato and Cicero sayings that are very wise and very beautiful; but I never read in either of them, "Come unto me, all ye that labor and are heavy laden."
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895)

text: As the soul is the life of the body, so God is the life of the soul. As therefore the body perishes when the soul leaves it, so the soul dies when God departs from it.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895)

text: Christ is not valued at all unless He be valued above all.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895)

text: It is not by change of place that we can come nearer to Him who is in every place, but by the cultivation of pure desires and virtuous habits.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895)

text: Give, O Lord, what Thou commandest, and then command what Thou wilt.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895)

text: Thou hast made us for Thyself, and the heart never resteth till it findeth rest in Thee.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895)

text: It is no advantage to be near the light if the eyes are closed.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895)

text: The true servants of God are not solicitous that He should order them to do what they desire to do, but that they may desire to do what He orders them to do.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895)

text: Vain is thy service, and false thy devotion to the Head, for to sever it from the body injures both.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: He who disdained not to assume us unto Himself, did not disdain to take our place and speak our words, in order that we might speak His words.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: Say nothing without Him, and He will say nothing without thee.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: God's greatest gift: uniting us to Christ, His Word, as members to a Head. He prays for us, in us, and is prayed to by us, making us one with Him.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: Who is this one man crying from the ends of the earth? He is one, but that one is unity, crying from all, in all.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: Christ's whole body groans in pain, a ceaseless cry to God. Each of us, a member, joins this one man's voice, reaching unto the end of time.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: Christ still suffers, not in His ascended flesh, but in mine, making up what is lacking to His sufferings on earth.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: The passion of Christ is not in Christ alone; it continues in His members, the Church, until the end of the world, as we fill up what is lacking in His sufferings.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: When the Head and members are despised, then the whole Christ is despised, for the whole Christ, Head and body, is that just man against whom deceitful lips speak iniquity (Ps. 30:19).
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: O sons of Peace, sons of the One Catholic [Church], walk in your way, and sing as you walk. Travelers do this in order to keep up their spirits.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: We can say 'I am holy,' not in pride, but in gratitude for the holiness received through Christ, our Head, with whom we are in inseparable union.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: Let the whole body of Christ cry out, despite tribulations: 'Preserve my soul, for I am holy.' This holy one is not proud, for he trusts in God.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: The members of Christ, many though they be, are bound to one another by the ties of charity and peace under the one Head, who is our Saviour Himself, and form one man. Often their voice is heard in the Psalms as the voice of one man ; the cry of one is as the cry of all, for all are one in One.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: The Word takes to Himself one man, for He takes unity; his Head is Christ. This one man, both many and one, becomes God's temple, and in Him, we are assumed and chosen.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: Christ, Head of the Church, hallows Himself for His members, sanctifying them in Himself, for 'they too are Myself,' that they may be hallowed in truth, which is Christ Himself.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: We are He , since we are His body and since He was made man in order to be our Head.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: We are members of this Head, and this body cannot be decapitated . If the Head is in glory forever, so too are the members in glory forever, that Christ may be undivided forever.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: In this one man, the whole Church has been assumed by the Word.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: God's incomprehensible love for us existed before the world was made, desiring us to be His sons with His Only-begotten Son.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: Love all men, even your enemies; love them not for who they are, but that they may become your brothers, thus extending fraternal love to all.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: It is vain to honor Christ as Head while despising His body, His members on earth, for He remains on earth through them.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Unknown
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: If you love the Head, you must also love the members; if you love not the members, neither do you love the Head.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: I would not believe the Gospel unless the authority of the Catholic Church moved me.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Nicene Post-Nicene Fathers translation: "I should not believe the gospel except as moved by the authority of the Catholic Church."

text: Humility makes men like holy angels; pride makes demons of angels.
author: Saint Augustine
source: It was pride that changed angels into devils ; it is humility that makes men as angels. As quoted in Manipulus Florum ( c. 1306), edited by Thomas Hibernicus , Superbia i cum uariis; also in Best Thoughts Of Best Thinkers: Amplified, Classified, Exemplified and Arranged as a Key to unlock the Literature of All Ages (1904) edited by Hialmer Day Gould and Edward Louis Hessenmueller

text: Through her gentle virtues and silent endurance, my mother won my violent father to Christ.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Reported in Josiah Hotchkiss Gilbert, Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895), p. 351

text: Charity is no substitute for justice withheld.
author: Saint Augustine
source: As quoted in Majority of One (1957) by Sydney J. Harris, p. 283

text: Love manifests as hands that help, feet that hasten to the needy, eyes that see misery, and ears that hear sorrow.
author: Saint Augustine
source: As quoted in Quote, Unquote (1977) by Lloyd Cory, p. 197

text: Hope has two beautiful daughters. Their names are anger and courage; anger at the way things are, and courage to see that they do not remain the way they are.
author: Saint Augustine
source: As quoted in Spirituality and Liberation: Overcoming the Great Fallacy (1988) by Robert McAfee Brown, p. 136

text: To wisdom belongs the intellectual apprehension of things eternal; to knowledge, the rational apprehension of things temporal.
author: Saint Augustine
source: As quoted in The Anchor Book of Latin Quotations: with English translations‎ (1990) by Norbert Guterman, p. 375

text: By faithfulness we are collected and wound up into unity within ourselves, whereas we had been scattered abroad in multiplicity.

text: Do not despair: one thief was saved. Do not presume: one thief was damned.

text: Without God, we cannot. Without us, God will not.

text: If you believe what you like in the gospels, and reject what you don't like, it is not the gospel you believe, but yourself.

text: To my God a heart of flame; To my fellow man a heart of love; To myself a heart of steel.

text: In essentials, unity; in non-essentials, liberty; in all things, charity.

source: In necessary things, unity ; in doubtful things, liberty ; in all things, charity ( love ). The first known occurrence of such an expression is as " Omnesque mutuam amplecteremur unitatem in necessariis, in non necessariis libertatem, in omnibus caritatem " in De Republica Ecclesiastica by Marco Antonio de Dominis , Pars I. London (1617), lib. 4 cap. 8 p. 676 (penultimate sentence) books.google , cf. liberlocorumcommunium .

text: The world is a great book, of which they that never stir from home read only a page.

text: There is no saint without a past, no sinner without a future.
author: Saint Augustine
source: This is sometimes attributed to Augustine, but the earliest known occurrence is in Persian Rosary (c. 1929) by Ahmad Sohrab (PDF) , which probably originates as a paraphrase of a statement in Oscar Wilde 's 1893 play A Woman of No Importance : "The only difference between the saint and the sinner is that every saint has a past and every sinner has a future."

text: The truth is like a lion. You don’t have to defend it. Let it loose and it will defend itself.

text: Prostitution in towns is like the sewer in a palace; take away the sewers and the palace becomes an impure and stinking place.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: This quote, frequently attributed to Aquinas, is actually a paraphrase of a passage (itself an elaborate paraphrase of Augustine ) by Ptolemy of Lucca in his continuation of an unfinished work by Aquinas. The passage from Ptolemy reads: "Thus, Augustine says that a whore acts in the world as the bilge in a ship or the sewer in a palace: 'Remove the sewer, and you will fill the palace with a stench.' Similarly, concerning the bilge, he says: 'Take away whores from the world, and you will fill it with sodomy.'" (Ptolemy of Lucca and Thomas Aquinas, On the Government of Rulers , trans. James M. Blythe. Philadelphia: University of Pennsylvania Press, 1997, 4. 14. 6). What Augustine actually wrote (in De ordine , 2. 4. 12) was simply: "Remove prostitutes from human affairs and you will unsettle everything on account of lusts." Only Book 1 and the first four chapters of Book 2 of On the Government of Rulers ( De Regimine Principum ) are by Aquinas. The rest of the work was written by Ptolemy. (It even mentions the coronation of Albert I of Hapsburg, an event that occurred in 1298, twenty-four years after Aquinas's death.) The quote comes from Book 4, which was definitely not written by Aquinas.
